text:
While AAOI’s primary business is building traditional pluggable optical transceivers, they also develop foundational high-power lasers and external laser sources for CPO architectures. So they’re bridging the gap today while also prepping for what comes next. CPO is a 2028/29 story at best. But the point is having a fundamental basis to justify a stock price today. Whether on a visible discounted cash flow basis or just material revenue/backlog growth TODAY. AAOI has explosive revenue growth actually materializing, on top of a surging **backlog**, whereas Sivers is baiting retail with a merely subjective **opportunity pipeline**, with zero photonics revenue today, let alone even a backlog to show for anything. Just non-exclusive partnerships on reference designs that they hope will be used by 2028. And pricing power will be fully at the discretion of WIN Semi, who they outsource designs to, further eroding potential margins should anything even manifest. I mean hey, this thing could surely pump again, but the risks are IMMENSE and it will only run on hype, which is intrinsically fizzling out (especially with all this dilution and some serious macro risks on the horizon/liquidity tightening). I suspect some funky business going on with their management, and I’m merely trying to make people aware not to blindly follow anonymous pump pages

text:
Scaling silicon photonics from the lab to high-volume manufacturing requires more than precision. It demands automation, repeatability, and throughput.

FormFactor TRITON™,  the industry's first fully integrated silicon photonics test cell, combines optical and electrical wafer test, nano-precision alignment, and factory automation to help manufacturers scale production with confidence.

text:
Paradis Labs ยังมั่นใจ $AAOI และ $AEHR แม้หุ้น AI Small Cap ปรับฐานแรง

Paradis Labs ระบุว่ายังคงถือหุ้น AAOI และ AEHR พร้อมแสดงความเชื่อมั่นต่อแนวโน้มระยะยาวของทั้งสองบริษัท แม้ว่าหุ้นกลุ่ม AI Infrastructure ขนาดเล็กจะถูกแรงขายกดดันในช่วงที่ผ่านมา

สำหรับ AAOI มองว่า

ตั้งเป้ารายได้จาก Optical Transceiver แตะประมาณ 471 ล้านดอลลาร์ต่อเดือนภายในกลางปี 2027
บริษัทกำลังขยายกำลังการผลิตรองรับผลิตภัณฑ์ 800G และ 1.6T
แม้เพิ่มกำลังผลิตแล้ว คาดว่าอุปสงค์จะยังสูงกว่ากำลังการผลิตจนถึงประมาณไตรมาส 3 ปี 2027

ส่วน AEHR

เพิ่งได้รับคำสั่งซื้อเพิ่มเติมสำหรับระบบ Wafer Level Burn-In (WLBI) จากลูกค้า Silicon Photonics
มีโอกาสได้รับคำสั่งซื้อเพิ่มภายในปีนี้ หลังผ่านการรับรองเป็นซัพพลายเออร์แล้ว
ก่อนหน้านี้ยังได้รับคำสั่งซื้อระบบ Package Level Burn-In (PLBI) จากลูกค้า Hyperscaler รายใหญ่

Paradis Labs ระบุว่า หุ้นทั้งสองตัวมีความผันผวนสูง (High Beta) จึงถูกแรงขายในรอบ AI Selloff มากกว่าหุ้นขนาดใหญ่ แต่ยังเชื่อมั่นในแนวโน้มระยะยาวและยังคงถือการลงทุนต่อ

I have high conviction in $AAOI and $AEHR.

And still hold my positions.

With $AAOI:
> Target ~$471M monthly transceiver revenue mid-2027.
> Capacity ramp is happening now for 800G/1.6T.
> Even with capacity expansion, demand is expected to exceed production capacity until ~Q3

text:
Be warned!! This is a little long but VERY informative on who Kopin should be prospecting and timeline of execution.

I KNOW we are early on $KOPN with its new MicroLED Neural I/o chip design. And its DEMO to be presented end of 2026. So trying to take advantage while their is time, #KOPIN should be looking into hyperscalers first. 

Hyperscalers & Cloud AI Builders (Top Priority)

NVIDIA (GPU clusters, NVLink complements), Google (TPUs), Amazon (Trainium/Inferentia), Microsoft (Azure Maia/custom silicon), Meta, Oracle, xAI, and others building massive AI training/inference clusters.

WHY? 
They face acute power and bandwidth walls in dense GPU racks. Power/cooling is a top TCO driver; Neural I/o promises major efficiency gains for intra-rack or short inter-rack GPU-to-GPU and GPU-to-memory traffic.
AI Chip/Accelerator Designers
NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom (custom ASICs), and startups (Cerebras, Groq, SambaNova, etc.). Also advanced packaging players (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) for chiplet or co-packaged optics (CPO) integration.

WHY? 
Direct integration into next-gen accelerators or 2.5D/3D packages. Supports high-parallelism needs without heavy SerDes bottlenecks.

Server/System OEMs & Integrators Supermicro, Dell, HPE, Lenovo.

WHY? 
They build the racks/servers sold to hyperscalers. Better interconnects enable higher-density, lower-power systems that differentiate their offerings. Networking & Photonics Ecosystem Broadcom (AVGO—networking ASICs/optics leader), Arista, Cisco, plus photonics firms (Lumentum/LITE, Coherent). Potential partners or complementors rather than pure competitors for specific short-reach use cases.

Defense, Government & Sovereign AI Infrastructure

DoD/intelligence community data centers, contractors, and sovereign AI projects (Europe, Middle East, Asia) seeking secure/domestic supply chains.

WHY?
Kopin’s US-based full MicroLED production capability + long defense heritage (displays, soldier systems) provides a strong moat for secure AI hardware. Dual-use potential with existing defense MicroLED work.

Advantages of $KOPN vs. Current Data Center Interconnects

Neural I/o sits in a “sweet spot” between copper’s limitations and traditional laser optics’ drawbacks for short-to-medium reach, high-bandwidth AI intra-cluster links. It draws from “wide-and-slow” parallel channel concepts (hundreds/thousands of lower-speed MicroLED channels instead of a few ultra-high-speed lanes), similar to Microsoft Research’s MOSAIC project (which demonstrated ~56-68% lower power, up to 50m reach, and much higher reliability using MicroLEDs + imaging fiber).Neural I/o wins on bandwidth scalability (parallelism enables 1.6 Tbps demo target soon, 3.6 Tbps roadmap), dramatically lower power per bit, less heat (easier cooling, higher rack density), and better reach for board/rack scales. It “breaks the copper wall” while keeping costs and power closer to copper-like economics for these reaches.Vs. Traditional Optical (InP/VCSEL lasers, silicon photonics transceivers, pluggable optics or CPO):
Lasers excel at longer reach but are power-hungry, expensive, thermally sensitive, complex (heavy DSP, CDR, FEC, equalization, SerDes), and overkill (plus higher failure rates) for many short intra-rack or chip-to-chip links in AI clusters. They add cost, power, and complexity where not fully needed.

Neural I/o wins by using MicroLEDs as direct emitters (cheaper, lower power, more temperature-robust, simpler drive electronics possible with parallel low-speed channels). It eliminates or reduces need for expensive laser modules and complex digital backends for targeted use cases. Potential for smaller form factor, higher reliability/redundancy (pixel-level), and better power efficiency (comparable to MOSAIC’s ~3-5W vs. 10-12W per link range in research). Bidirectional capability from NeuralDisplay architecture is a plus. It avoids “expensive laser-based systems entirely” for these applications while delivering needed bandwidth.

LOOONG $KOPN

text:
Daily Buzzberg Alpha

the tape isn't sending one macro signal. it's repricing a regime: geopolitical supply risk returning while ai capex becomes an earnings tailwind AND an inflation/rates risk at the same time.

that combination kills generic beta and creates dispersion. the market has stopped paying for ai exposure or risk-on exposure — it's paying for contracted demand, validated supply-chain position, and balance-sheet capacity. it's punishing crowded trades where the thesis is intact but the holder base is impaired.

the whole note reduces to one filter: contracts > stories.

— where the asymmetry sits —

$WYFI — cleanest setup in the neocloud wreckage. ~$1b+ contracted backlog vs ~$1.3b market cap, and nscale's $900m credit facility validates the counterparty. sold with the basket, priced like the contracts don't exist.

$AVGO — the one ai print this week backed by a signed deal: apple's $30b+ agreement + $1.5b colorado buildout. validates the custom asic layer beyond gpu scarcity. revenue visibility, not vibes — and the cleaner read-through than $AAPL, which carries higher domestic cost and eu overhang.

$ONDS — thesis tied to numbers, not narrative: path to ~$1b revenue by 2027, net-income inflection, sum-of-parts floor, q2 print as the catalyst.

photonics — the under-the-surface one. inp substrates easing from "very tight" to "tight" isn't bearish — it lets downstream optical names convert interconnect demand into shipments. distinguish upstream bottleneck vs downstream qualification moats. $LITE $AAOI $COHR

$RKLB — flow unusually specific: 200dma entry, ms overweight $105 pt, vertical-integration bull case. blue origin's $130b private raise now anchors every public space comp.

— crowded, entry decayed —

$PENG — textbook signal decay. real discovery a month ago (earnings beat, cxl exposure, nvidia ai factory partner). now the flow is victory laps and partial trims. thesis quality high, entry quality gone.

broad ai beta — warsh's divided fomc tied ai capex to upside inflation risk, some members already floating hikes. that's the mechanism: the buildout that feeds suppliers also keeps real rates sticky, so long-duration ai without contract visibility underperforms. $TLT stays exposed.

— battleground —

memory — highest-dispersion area on the tape. korean flow reads the samsung/hynix selloff as forced deleveraging (levered-etf + retail-credit mechanics, adr re-rating catalyst) with hbm4/rubin demand structural through 2028. us flow is chaos — "memory is dead" next to aggressive $MU $SNDK dip buys. right framing: thesis intact, ownership base damaged. tradable, not clean.

oil — the move isn't the crude print, it's the transmission. brent ~$80 is the tactical level where short-covering extends, but the real signal is hormuz uncertainty leaking into diesel, propane, shipping and reserves — inflation pass-through that survives even if front-month crude stalls. own the complex where the physical market stays tight. $USO $CRAK

also on the desk: $NBIS washed-out sub-$170 but the bar is now contracted utilization, $META's $10b canada dc arguing against a capex stop, $OUST physical-ai perception via nvidia jetson, $HOOD chain optionality (early, distribution call not revenue).

bottom line: the tape is moving from "ai trade" to "ai underwriting." it pays receipts and punishes stories — $AVGO and $WYFI have them, $PENG had them last month, memory has them for 2028 but not for this week.

text: Nvidia $NVDA CEO Jensen Huang has announced a litany of partnerships over the past year focused on data centers, photonics technology, and more. I compiled a list of those partnerships and why each matters. https://t.co/D2NBUD74Uj

The Big Five are spending on AI faster than they're earning: @Microsoft, @Google, @amazon, @Meta. If CapEx exceeds cash flow, it can only persist as long as investors keep funding it. @PeterDiamandis asked me: What happens if sentiment shifts?

Here's my take: sentiment won’t shift. If anything, the investment will become more justified over time.

The world still sees compute as a rapidly depreciating asset but that’s simply not what it is anymore. Short of a massive leap in photonics or quantum computing, GPUs will continue to be highly sought after and have a predictable service life. This is already turning GPUs hours into a commodity.

Think about it like buying a house: when you buy a house, you finance it, and the house is collateral. In a world where compute becomes acceptable collateral, the hyperscalers could easily raise 10x to 100x on their cash flow in equity and debt, if they choose to.

In reality, there’s a lot of investable dollars in the world that want to flow into AI but can’t. AI is still the largest wealth creation moment in the history of humankind.

If CapEx exceeds cash flow, it can only persist as long as investors keep funding it. @PeterDiamandis asked me: What happens if sentiment shifts? Here's my take: sentiment won’t shift. If anything, the investment will become more justified over time. The world still sees compute as a rapidly depreciating asset but that’s simply not what it is anymore. Short of a massive leap in photonics or quantum computing, GPUs will continue to be highly sought after and have a predictable service life. This is already turning GPUs hours into a commodity. Think about it like buying a house: when you buy a house, you finance it, and the house is collateral. In a world where compute becomes acceptable collateral, the hyperscalers could easily raise 10x to 100x on their cash flow in equity and debt, if they choose to. In reality, there’s a lot of investable dollars in the world that want to flow into AI but can’t. text:
Spot on. $SIVE is textbook.

Retail got positioned early on the AI photonics thesis, institutions finally show up (JP Morgan 5%+), then boom, dilution shakeout + noise drops it 65% from the highs while the pipeline and partnerships keep firing.

Swedish sellers out, US buyers (and big money) stepping in on the cheap. Classic transfer.
The narrative isn’t dead, it’s just getting loaded quietly. AI data center optics demand isn’t pausing.
Backing up the truck on these dips.

text:
Institutional Lenders Just Swapped $12M in Debt for $SIVE Equity at the Bottom. If You’re Selling $SIVE Here, You’re Trading Against the Smart Money.

Until a month ago, everyone was bullish on $SIVE at the top. Now everyone is max bearish because it’s down 55% from ATH. Classic retail psychology. People see red, panic, and completely miss the structural reality of what’s actually happening beneath the surface for $SIVE.

If you are short $SIVE here, you're basically betting the entire AI data center physical layout is going to grind to a halt. But look at how the puzzle pieces are locking together right now for $SIVE:

The Silicon Valley Pure-Play Carve-Out: The crowd is totally blind to this value play. $SIVE is merging its hyper-growth photonics business with Nordic Acquisition to spin it off into a standalone, U.S. Nasdaq-listed entity. By liberating this unit into the heart of the tech ecosystem, $SIVE is putting its indispensable Indium Phosphide (InP) laser platform right in front of deep-pocketed American AI capital while keeping controlling ownership.

The Institutional Vote of Confidence: Skeptics keep crying about paper dilution, but they completely ignored what just crossed the wire. A top-tier institutional lender (Bootstrap Europe) chose to convert their entire $12M loan straight into ordinary $SIVE equity at these exact lower levels. Smart money didn't want their cash back; they took 22 million shares of equity instead because they know the optical supplier thesis is fully intact.

The Weaponized Balance Sheet: That recent SEK 700 million directed share issue wasn’t generic fundraising, it was a calculated move. Equipment lead times for high-purity cleanrooms are hovering at 9 to 12 months. $SIVE proactively secured the cash today to expand its InP manufacturing capacity. If you aren’t spending that CAPEX right now, you completely miss the 2027 demand curve.

The Reality Check: Near-term execution risks, corporate restructuring friction, and high beta mean localized volatility for $SIVE will stay extreme. Microcaps transitioning into multi-entity setups always face messy trailing quarters before peak commercial production volume ramps late next year.

But if you look past the standard retail panic, $SIVE just systematically wiped out its external debt, capitalized its capacity expansions, and established a direct pathway to Silicon Valley valuation multiples. This isn't a structural breakdown for $SIVE, it is a massive accumulation window.

Holding my core $SIVE positions without a single trim.

As demonstrated by the parabolic re-rating of optical infrastructure stocks such as Lumentum ($LITE – Buy, $1,142 PT ) and Coherent ($COHR – Neutral, $309 PT ), the prevailing investment thesis assumed an aggressive, compounding multiplier: scaling AI clusters meant building deeper, multi-tiered networks (3-tier or 4-tier Clos topologies), exponentially driving up transceiver demand per unit of compute. This thesis, however, is hitting a structural wall. Just a year after the 2025 explosion, the simultaneous market introduction of Amazon’s ($AMZN, NR) Resilient Network Graphs (RNG) and OpenAI’s Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) protocol marks a major pivot toward network flattening across both training and inference workloads. Microsoft ($MSFT, NR) and Oracle ($ORCL, NR) are actively integrating the open-source MRC protocol into their frontier AI training supercomputing clusters—such as ORCL’s massive GB200 Abilene site—to execute sender-controlled packet spraying and bypass physical network bottlenecks. Concurrently, AMZN is deploying its quasi-random RNG architecture across its general-purpose cloud fabric. While RNG is isolated from back-end training clusters, it serves as the primary backbone for scale-out generative AI inference, data ingestion, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Crucially, as mature AI models transition from training to mass inference, hyperscaler capex is shifting heavily in favor of these front-end, scale-out environments. Because RNG replaces traditional, active multi-tiered aggregation switches with passive optical ShuffleBoxes, it reduces active networking equipment by over 60%. Consequently, as AI workloads migrate to inference, this architectural deflation loops directly into the broader optical market, stripping out switch-to-switch optical links and dismantling the legacy transceiver-to-compute multiplier. For the transceiver market, this dual-front flattening represents a severe structural headwind that will sharply decelerate the sector's long-term TAM expansion, since these technologies reduce structural transceiver counts by 40% to 50%. We believe Applied Optoelectronics ($AAOI – Neutral, $129 PT) will be especially vulnerable to this development. AMZN and ORCL—the primary network provider to OpenAI—are expected to be the anchor customers for the 800G/1.6T transceivers forecast to drive AAOI's quarterly revenue to ~$1B by 2H27. As ORCL deflates training-cluster transceiver ratios via MRC, and AMZN strips active transceiver requirements from inference rollouts via RNG, AAOI's core revenue growth engines face massive structural risk.""}}}, "rest_id": "2074071756109656328", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "427477", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2074282003319452014", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "53", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$sive is real!

우리에게 생소할 수 있지만, 글로벌 데이터센터, 클라우드, 인프라 업계에서는 매우 높은 신뢰도와 영향력을 가진 대표적인 전문 B2B 미디어인 ‘Data Center Frontier'라는 곳에서,

AI 클러스터가 거대해짐에 따라 기존 구리선 기반 연결의 한계를 극복하기 위해 광학 기술 및 실리콘 포토닉스를 데이터센터 인프라에 도입해야 한다는 내용을 다룸. 

그리고 이 기사는 6시간 전에 올라온 기사.

이 기사는 데이터 센터 프론티어의 시니어 에디터인 David Chernicoff가 작성했으며,

중요한 건 $sive 관련 내용이 ‘굵직하게’ 포함됨. 

총 5가지 내용 중에 심지어 한 부분을 차지함. 

(원문 발췌)
“Those developments are now being reinforced by a broader series of moves across the optical ecosystem:
- Corning’s major AI infrastructure partnerships with NVIDIA and Amazon,
- GlobalFoundries’ push into co-packaged optics,
- Sivers’ laser-array collaboration with GlobalFoundries, $sive
- Wiwynn’s co-packaged optics demonstration at Computex,
- Credo’s acquisition of DustPhotonics, and emerging near-packaged optical interconnect designs from LightSpeed Photonics.”

기사의 $sive 관련 세부내용으로는,

1. $sive와 글로벌 파운드리스 $gfs 와의 레이저 어레이 협력 명시

- Sivers의 레이저 어레이(Laser arrays)는 글로벌파운드리의 실리콘 포토닉스 플랫폼을 기반으로 제작된 레퍼런스 디자인(참조 설계)에 통합되며, GF의 SCALE 광학 엔진 솔루션으로 제공될 예정. 

2. Wiwynn의 CPO 안의 Ayar labs 명시

- 알다시피 Ayar labs의 SuperNova에 $sive 가 들어감

이 두 내용을 이런 미디어의 기사에서 명시했다는 건 $sive 가 진짜라는 걸 간접적으로 확인해주는 것이지 않을까?

이 데이터 센터 프론티어라는 미디어는, 

독자층이 주로 구글, 마이크로소프트, 메타, AWS 같은 하이퍼스케일러(Hyperscaler)의 인프라 엔지니어링 임원, 데이터센터 아키텍트, 설계·건설 책임자, 그리고 데이터센터 리츠(REITs) 투자가들이라고 하고, 

독자의 약 50% 이상이 C-Level(경영진)이나 VP, 디렉터급으로 구성되어 있어, 실질적인 인프라 투자와 기술 도입에 중요한 이정표가 된다고 함. 

기사 원문 링크는 댓글 참고

text:
Be careful on $AAOI.

There's a crazy amount of resentment from institutions such as B. Riley who MISSED the stock and were bearish as low as $15.

Now making a massive intellectual leap on a nonsensical optical short thesis and literally name dropping $AAOI out of bitterness? Spite? For the virality?

You really can't make up the misinformation and FUD in photonics right now it's just unbelievable. Earnings should put everyone in their place very soon. Nothing has changed in the fact that there isn't enough photonics tech in the world today and won't be for years.

Everyone in my paid Substack + Discord knows I've been buying $AAOI all the way down from $160 in my trading portfolio, most recently loading at $114. I also increased $AAOI in my "Photonics Is Next" portfolio today.

No asymmetry without FUD - NAWF.

RILEY SAYS AMAZON'S RNG & OPENAI'S MRC PROTOCOLS POSE MAJOR STRUCTURAL RISK FOR OPTICAL INFRASTRUCTURE / "PHOTONICS" STOCKS, NAMELY APPLIED OPTOELECTRONICS $AAOI Full comments: "Following the OFC conference in March 2025, the AI data center boom triggered an unprecedented optical supercycle. As demonstrated by the parabolic re-rating of optical infrastructure stocks such as Lumentum ($LITE – Buy, $1,142 PT ) and Coherent ($COHR – Neutral, $309 PT ), the prevailing investment thesis assumed an aggressive, compounding multiplier: scaling AI clusters meant building deeper, multi-tiered networks (3-tier or 4-tier Clos topologies), exponentially driving up transceiver demand per unit of compute. This thesis, however, is hitting a structural wall. Just a year after the 2025 explosion, the simultaneous market introduction of Amazon’s ($AMZN, NR) Resilient Network Graphs (RNG) and OpenAI’s Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) protocol marks a major pivot toward network flattening across both training and inference workloads. Microsoft ($MSFT, NR) and Oracle ($ORCL, NR) are actively integrating the open-source MRC protocol into their frontier AI training supercomputing clusters—such as ORCL’s massive GB200 Abilene site—to execute sender-controlled packet spraying and bypass physical network bottlenecks. Concurrently, AMZN is deploying its quasi-random RNG architecture across its general-purpose cloud fabric. While RNG is isolated from back-end training clusters, it serves as the primary backbone for scale-out generative AI inference, data ingestion, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Crucially, as mature AI models transition from training to mass inference, hyperscaler capex is shifting heavily in favor of these front-end, scale-out environments. Because RNG replaces traditional, active multi-tiered aggregation switches with passive optical ShuffleBoxes, it reduces active networking equipment by over 60%. Consequently, as AI workloads migrate to inference, this architectural deflation loops directly into the broader optical market, stripping out switch-to-switch optical links and dismantling the legacy transceiver-to-compute multiplier. For the transceiver market, this dual-front flattening represents a severe structural headwind that will sharply decelerate the sector's long-term TAM expansion, since these technologies reduce structural transceiver counts by 40% to 50%. We believe Applied Optoelectronics ($AAOI – Neutral, $129 PT) will be especially vulnerable to this development. AMZN and ORCL—the primary network provider to OpenAI—are expected to be the anchor customers for the 800G/1.6T transceivers forecast to drive AAOI's quarterly revenue to ~$1B by 2H27. As ORCL deflates training-cluster transceiver ratios via MRC, and AMZN strips active transceiver requirements from inference rollouts via RNG, AAOI's core revenue growth engines face massive structural risk.""}}}, "rest_id": "2074071756109656328", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "427477", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2074273679697961422", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "134136", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
An ETF shop just filed a pure-play photonics ETF. That tells you as much about the top as it does about the opportunity.

$LYTE is Roundhill's new Photonics and Optics ETF. It is filed with the SEC but not trading yet. When it launches it will hold the optics pure-plays powering AI data centers: names like Lumentum, Coherent, POET, AAOI, IPG, and Ciena.

The pitch writes itself. If you believe in the photonics buildout but you cannot say whether $LITE, $COHR, or $POET wins, you own the group, not the single-name risk. That is a good argument for the basket.

But two things are worth saying out loud.

First, both the ETF and the single names are falling knives right now. This is a theme-wide de-rating. LITE knifed lower, AXTI is down 54% off its high, the whole optics complex is bleeding together. An ETF spreads single-name risk. It does nothing when the entire group falls. And LYTE is not even technically diversified. Its own filing calls it non-diversified, a concentrated basket of a handful of optics names.

Second, thematic ETFs tend to launch near tops. Roundhill built this because its DRAM ETF was a smash, hitting $1B in 10 days. Then memory crashed. An issuer racing to package a hot theme right as it corrects is a yellow flag, not a green light.

The move is not ETF or stock. It is neither, yet. Wait for the optics group to base, then use both: the ETF as the diversified core once it trades, the single name as the tactical piece you can sell puts on.

Not financial advice.

Photonics
Neoclouds

The pieces of the supply chain that have to exist for the entire stack to work.

That's the difference between someone actually doing homework and someone just drawing lines on a chart.

We are seeking a highly motivated and enthusiastic Electronics Engineer to join our team and contribute to our mission in accelerating the growth and adoption of integrated photonics technologies.

▫️Job Title: Electronics Engineer
▫️Job Type: Full-time
▫️Salary: £50k – £70k pa., depending on experience
▫️Location: Cambridge, East of England, England
▫️Start Date: August 2026 or ASAP

📍Visa Sponsorship is available to those already in the UK 🇬🇧 

Qualifications 
✅️ Degree in Electronic Engineering, Electrical Engineering, Physics, or a related discipline.
✅️ Strong software development skills for embedded programming in C.
✅️ Python/MATLAB experience for measurement automation, data analysis, test and verification.
✅️ Commitment to facilitate effective cross-team knowledge transfer through clear documentation.

Apply here: https://t.co/Fq5Hqn0QQr

Please note: This Job is open to only those currently in the UK. Those outside the UK should not apply 

Please share this with others in the UK who may be Interested

 30-Second Stock Market Review

1️⃣ $META up 3% on a positive SemiAnalysis report stating that capex for 2027 is likely to remain intact, as the AI thesis remains solid. 
2️⃣ $BE up 8% amid ongoing volatility after the company recently announced a $25B framework from Brookfield to expand AI infrastructure. 
3️⃣ $AMD up 6% after Goldman Sachs raised its price target to $640. 
4️⃣ $IREN up 13% as AI compute and crypto stocks rallied. 
5️⃣ $TSLA up 6% amid recent production and delivery figures, along with momentum in robotics and physical AI stocks. 
6️⃣ $TSM up 4% on reports that the company is expected to raise its full-year revenue outlook to nearly 40% growth, up from 30%.
7️⃣ $CRDO up 10% as stocks in the optics and photonics sectors had relief rallies on Monday. 
8️⃣ $RKLB down 7% as space-themed stocks traded broadly lower. 
9️⃣ $DELL up 4% on positive comments from President Trump, urging consumers to buy the company’s products. 
🔟 $WULF up 4% on news that the company executed a 20-year lease deal with Anthropic at its data campus site in Kentucky. Also, the company signed an agreement to sell a 50.1% stake in the Abernathy joint venture to an investor group.

Market News

🆕 Bitcoin rose to nearly $64K on Monday after Trump made positive comments on crypto. 
🆕 Semiconductor stocks rallied after a nearly two-week slump.

text:
Quantum Technology: A New Kind of Technology

Quantum technology uses the strange behavior of extremely small particles to create powerful new tools.

Scientists are developing quantum computers, communication systems, and sensors that may someday improve medicine, cybersecurity, navigation, energy, and scientific research.

What Is a Quantum Computer?

Ordinary computers use bits that represent either a zero or a one.

Quantum computers use quantum bits, called qubits. Qubits can behave in unusual ways that may allow a quantum computer to examine certain difficult problems much faster than an ordinary computer.

This does not mean quantum computers will replace phones, laptops, or regular computers. They are being designed for special problems that are extremely difficult to solve.

How Could Quantum Computers Help?

Quantum computers may eventually help scientists:

Discover new medicines
Design stronger materials
Improve batteries
Study complicated molecules
Plan transportation routes
Develop cleaner energy
Understand diseases
Improve weather and climate research

Many of these possibilities are still being tested. Quantum computers are not yet powerful or reliable enough to perform most of these tasks better than ordinary computers.

Why Are Quantum Computers So Difficult to Build?

Qubits are extremely fragile.

Heat, vibration, electrical interference, and tiny manufacturing errors can disturb them. Some quantum computers must be kept at temperatures colder than outer space.

Scientists are working to reduce errors and keep quantum information stable long enough to complete useful calculations.

Quantum Sensors

Quantum sensors may become useful sooner than large quantum computers.

These sensors can make extremely precise measurements. They may help doctors see inside the body, help ships or aircraft navigate when GPS is unavailable, locate objects underground, monitor the environment, and improve scientific research.

Atomic clocks are an example of quantum technology already being used today. They help support GPS, communications, banking, and other important systems.

Quantum Communication

Quantum communication may provide new ways to protect sensitive information.

It can use the laws of quantum physics to reveal whether someone has attempted to intercept a message.

However, no communication system is completely secure. Computers, software, passwords, and human mistakes can still create weaknesses.

Could Quantum Computers Break Encryption?

A powerful future quantum computer may be able to break some of the encryption currently protecting websites, financial systems, government information, and private communications.

Present quantum computers cannot do this on a large scale.

Governments and technology companies are already developing new forms of encryption designed to withstand future quantum attacks. This is called post-quantum cryptography.

Is Quantum Technology Being Exaggerated?

There is genuine progress, although there is also a great deal of publicity and exaggeration.

Companies sometimes announce impressive numbers of qubits without clearly explaining how reliable those qubits are. A machine with many unstable qubits may be less useful than a smaller and more accurate machine.

Promises about instant medical cures, unbeatable artificial intelligence, or computers that solve every problem should be treated carefully.

Why Does This Matter?

Quantum technology could eventually improve medicine, strengthen cybersecurity, create better materials, support cleaner energy, and provide more accurate measuring tools.

It could also increase military competition, threaten older encryption, and give powerful countries or corporations greater technological control.

Quantum technology is not magic, and it is not ready to transform everyday life immediately. It is an important developing field that may gradually change science, security, medicine, and industry.

The wisest response is neither fear nor blind excitement. We should follow its progress carefully, prepare for its risks, and use its discoveries responsibly.

Quantum technology seeks to control some of the smallest and strangest features of nature and turn them into useful machines.

The field includes far more than futuristic computers. It also includes extraordinarily sensitive measuring instruments, highly accurate clocks, secure communication methods, and networks that may someday connect quantum devices across great distances.

The three most developed areas are quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum sensing. Each uses the laws of quantum mechanics differently, and each is progressing at a different speed.

What Makes Something Quantum?

Ordinary computers store and process information using bits. A bit represents either a zero or a one.

A quantum computer uses quantum bits, usually called qubits. Under controlled conditions, a qubit can exist in a combination of possible states known as superposition. Multiple qubits can also become entangled, meaning their properties are connected in ways that cannot be fully explained by treating each particle separately.

Quantum algorithms manipulate these combined possibilities through interference. Some possible answers are strengthened, while others are weakened or canceled. When the qubits are measured, however, the delicate quantum state collapses into an ordinary result.

This does not mean that a quantum computer simply tries every possible answer at once and instantly selects the correct one. That popular explanation is too simplistic. A useful quantum algorithm must be carefully designed so that interference makes the desired information more likely to appear when the system is measured.

The Problem of Quantum Fragility

Quantum states are extremely delicate.

Heat, vibration, electromagnetic interference, imperfect controls, microscopic manufacturing defects, and interactions with the surrounding environment can introduce errors. The loss of usable quantum behavior is known as decoherence.

Some quantum computers therefore operate inside elaborate refrigeration systems at temperatures close to absolute zero. Other designs use trapped ions, neutral atoms, photons, semiconductor devices, or other physical systems.

Each approach has advantages and weaknesses. Researchers have not yet established one universally superior form of quantum hardware.

The number of qubits also tells only part of the story. A machine containing many unreliable qubits may accomplish less than one containing fewer, higher-quality qubits. Important measurements include error rates, connectivity, operating speed, coherence time, and the complexity of circuits the machine can execute accurately.

Quantum Error Correction

A fully useful quantum computer will probably require quantum error correction.

Because unknown quantum information cannot simply be copied like an ordinary computer file, error correction must distribute information across groups of physical qubits. Together, these physical qubits form a more dependable logical qubit.

This process requires substantial hardware. Depending on the quality of the components and the error-correction method, one reliable logical qubit may require many physical qubits.

Progress in error correction is therefore often more meaningful than announcements based only on total qubit counts. The central challenge is not merely constructing a larger processor. It is making the entire system more reliable as it grows.

Quantum Computing

Quantum computers are specialized machines intended to perform certain calculations using quantum effects.

They are not expected to replace laptops, phones, conventional servers, or supercomputers. Most daily tasks—including word processing, video playback, email, accounting, web browsing, and database management—are handled more efficiently by ordinary computers.

The more realistic future is hybrid computing. Conventional processors will manage most of a workload, while a quantum processor may be assigned a narrow calculation for which it offers a genuine advantage.

IBM, for example, has published plans for closer integration between quantum systems and classical supercomputers, along with tools for monitoring and debugging hybrid workloads. These are company roadmaps rather than guarantees of when broad commercial advantage will arrive.

Promising Uses of Quantum Computers

Chemistry and medicine

Nature is quantum mechanical at the molecular level. Simulating molecules accurately becomes extremely difficult for classical computers as their complexity increases.

Future quantum computers may help scientists model chemical reactions, proteins, catalysts, and pharmaceutical compounds. This could support drug discovery, although quantum computers would not eliminate laboratory testing, clinical trials, safety reviews, or the biological complexity of disease.

Materials and energy

Quantum simulation may help researchers investigate improved batteries, superconductors, fertilizers, solar materials, industrial catalysts, and methods of capturing carbon.

Even modest improvements in catalysts could reduce the energy required by major industrial processes. The difficulty is reaching a scale and accuracy that outperform the best classical methods on genuinely valuable problems.

Optimization

Organizations face complicated scheduling, routing, manufacturing, investment, and supply-chain problems.

Quantum algorithms may eventually help with certain forms of optimization. However, many optimization claims remain experimental. Classical algorithms also continue to improve, so a quantum method must be compared with the strongest conventional alternative rather than an outdated one.

Scientific discovery

Quantum computers may become valuable research instruments for nuclear physics, high-energy physics, materials science, and other fields involving complex quantum systems.

The Department of Energy has identified quantum information science as relevant to materials research, environmental measurement, navigation, dark-matter searches, gravitational-wave detection, and other scientific challenges.

Artificial intelligence

Quantum computing and artificial intelligence may eventually complement one another. AI can help calibrate quantum devices, analyze experimental results, and search for improved control methods.

Quantum algorithms might someday accelerate selected machine-learning calculations. Claims that quantum computers will automatically make all AI dramatically more powerful remain unproven.

Quantum Communication

Quantum communication transfers or shares information using quantum states, often carried by photons.

One important concept is quantum key distribution. It can allow two parties to create encryption keys while detecting certain attempts to intercept the quantum transmission. Measurement disturbs quantum states, so eavesdropping can leave detectable evidence.

Quantum key distribution does not make an entire communication system invulnerable. Hardware, software, users, authentication systems, and network endpoints can still be attacked.

Researchers are also working toward quantum networks that could connect sensors, processors, memories, and communication devices. Entanglement might eventually be distributed across these networks, enabling capabilities that ordinary networks cannot reproduce. NIST is developing components, prototypes, and test beds for quantum networking.

A large-scale “quantum internet” remains a long-term research goal. It would not simply be a faster version of today’s internet. It would likely operate alongside classical networks and provide specialized functions.

Quantum Sensing

Quantum sensing may affect society sooner than large fault-tolerant quantum computing.

Quantum sensors use properties such as atomic energy levels, spin, interference, and entanglement to measure time, gravity, magnetic fields, acceleration, rotation, temperature, and other physical quantities with exceptional precision.

Some quantum-based instruments already exist. Atomic clocks support precise timekeeping, navigation, telecommunications, scientific measurement, and the operation of modern infrastructure.

Emerging quantum sensors may help locate underground structures, detect subtle magnetic signals, improve medical imaging, monitor environmental changes, measure movement without relying entirely on GPS, and conduct fundamental scientific experiments.

Quantum sensing often does not require the enormous error-corrected processors needed for universal quantum computing. This may allow useful sensing products to mature earlier.

Navigation Without GPS

Modern transportation, communications, finance, energy systems, and military operations depend heavily upon accurate timing and satellite navigation.

Quantum accelerometers, gyroscopes, clocks, and gravimeters could contribute to navigation systems that continue operating when GPS signals are blocked, jammed, unavailable, or unreliable.

Such systems would not necessarily make satellites unnecessary. They could provide an additional layer of resilience for aircraft, ships, emergency services, research vessels, infrastructure, and national defense.

The Encryption Challenge

A sufficiently powerful fault-tolerant quantum computer could threaten widely used public-key cryptographic systems.

These systems help protect websites, financial transactions, government communications, software updates, digital identities, and private records. The danger does not mean present quantum machines can suddenly break all modern encryption. They cannot.

The concern is partly long-term. An attacker could collect encrypted information today and retain it until future technology becomes capable of decrypting it. This is sometimes described as “harvest now, decrypt later.”

The principal defense is post-quantum cryptography: conventional mathematical encryption designed to resist attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

Moving to new cryptographic systems takes years because organizations must identify vulnerable systems, update software and hardware, test compatibility, replace long-lived equipment, and protect archived information. Preparation should therefore begin before a cryptographically dangerous quantum computer exists.

Quantum communication and post-quantum cryptography should not be confused. One uses quantum physical systems; the other generally runs on conventional computers.

Where the Field Stands in 2026

Quantum research has entered a period of large public and private investment.

In November 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy announced $625 million to renew five National Quantum Information Science Research Centers. IBM announced in June 2026 that it planned to invest more than $10 billion in quantum computing over five years. These commitments show strategic confidence in the field, although investment does not guarantee that every technical roadmap will be achieved on schedule.

Researchers are improving control systems, fabrication, modular architectures, networking, software, and error-correction techniques. Companies are also attempting to integrate quantum processors with classical high-performance computing.

At the same time, the field remains vulnerable to exaggerated announcements. Experimental demonstrations may solve artificial benchmark problems without producing immediate commercial value. Company claims can also face scientific dispute, as recent criticism of Microsoft’s topological-qubit research illustrates.

Responsible evaluation requires published evidence, independent replication, meaningful benchmarks, and comparison with the best available classical machines.

Major Obstacles

Noise and errors

Qubits lose information and accumulate errors rapidly. Useful computation may require far greater reliability than most present systems provide.

Scaling

A laboratory device containing a small number of controllable qubits is very different from an industrial system containing enough logical qubits for valuable fault-tolerant computation.

Manufacturing

Quantum hardware requires extraordinary precision. Tiny variations can affect the behavior of individual qubits and reduce production yield.

Cooling and infrastructure

Some architectures require complex cryogenic equipment, shielding, lasers, vacuum systems, control electronics, or specialized facilities.

Software

Quantum programming demands new algorithms, compilers, verification methods, and ways to divide work between quantum and classical processors.

Verification

A difficult question arises when a quantum computer produces a result that no classical computer can independently reproduce. Researchers need trustworthy methods to verify the calculation.

Cost

Quantum systems remain expensive to build, operate, maintain, and calibrate. Early access will probably be concentrated in governments, universities, cloud providers, and large companies.

Economic and Social Effects

Quantum technology may create demand for physicists, engineers, programmers, technicians, mathematicians, cybersecurity specialists, materials scientists, and manufacturing workers.

The field also requires people who can connect scientific research with medicine, chemistry, business, law, ethics, and public policy.

Not every worker will need a doctorate in quantum physics. A mature industry will also require conventional software development, electronics, refrigeration, photonics, fabrication, maintenance, education, sales, and regulatory expertise.

At the same time, unequal access could concentrate scientific and economic power among a small number of countries and companies. Public research, education, shared infrastructure, international standards, and responsible cloud access may help broaden participation.

Military and Geopolitical Concerns

Quantum technology has implications for secure communication, navigation, surveillance, sensing, codebreaking, submarine detection, and advanced materials.

This makes it strategically important to governments. It also increases the danger of secrecy, international rivalry, export restrictions, and an expensive technological arms race.

Quantum research has many peaceful uses, yet the same capability may have both civilian and military applications. Nations will need to protect legitimate security interests while maintaining enough scientific cooperation to support safety, verification, and responsible standards.

Ethical Questions

Quantum technology raises several important questions:

Who should control the most powerful systems?

How should governments prevent misuse without blocking beneficial research?

How should claims of “quantum advantage” be tested?

How can society avoid wasting public money on hype?

How should privacy be protected during the transition to quantum-resistant security?

How can smaller nations, universities, and businesses participate without becoming permanently dependent on a few providers?

These questions should be addressed while the technology is developing, rather than after its effects become difficult to reverse.

What Quantum Technology Will Not Do

Quantum technology will not make every computer program faster.

It will not instantly cure disease, predict the future, eliminate uncertainty, make artificial intelligence all-knowing, or solve every mathematical problem.

Quantum entanglement does not permit ordinary information to travel faster than light.

Quantum computers also do not test every possibility and effortlessly reveal the correct answer. Their advantages depend upon particular algorithms, suitable hardware, low error rates, and problems whose structure can be exploited quantum mechanically.

Any claim that ignores these limitations should be treated cautiously.

A Realistic Outlook

Quantum sensing and timing are likely to deliver practical benefits earlier and more widely than universal fault-tolerant quantum computing.

Quantum communication will probably develop through specialized networks, government systems, research links, and security-sensitive applications before becoming broadly available.

Quantum computing may progress through increasingly capable hybrid systems. The decisive milestone will not be a dramatic qubit announcement. It will be the repeated solution of useful problems more accurately, quickly, or economically than the best classical alternatives.

That transition may be gradual. Classical computers will also continue advancing, creating a moving target that quantum systems must surpass.

Why Quantum Technology Matters to Ordinary People

Most people may never own a quantum computer, just as most people do not own a supercomputer or an atomic clock.

They may still benefit from the technology indirectly.

Quantum tools could contribute to safer medicines, stronger materials, improved batteries, more accurate medical instruments, resilient navigation, earlier detection of underground hazards, better scientific models, and stronger digital security.

They could also create risks if governments and businesses fail to modernize encryption, test claims carefully, control military applications responsibly, or ensure fair access.

Quantum technology should therefore be approached with neither blind excitement nor automatic fear. It is a serious developing field with genuine achievements, extraordinary possibilities, unresolved scientific barriers, and a large amount of uncertainty.

Its eventual influence will depend not only upon what researchers are able to build, but also upon the wisdom with which society chooses to use it.

Quantum technology uses the strange behavior of extremely small particles to create powerful new tools.

Scientists are developing quantum computers, communication systems, and sensors that may someday improve medicine, cybersecurity, navigation, energy, and scientific research.

What Is a Quantum Computer?

Ordinary computers use bits that represent either a zero or a one.

Quantum computers use quantum bits, called qubits. Qubits can behave in unusual ways that may allow a quantum computer to examine certain difficult problems much faster than an ordinary computer.

This does not mean quantum computers will replace phones, laptops, or regular computers. They are being designed for special problems that are extremely difficult to solve.

How Could Quantum Computers Help?

Quantum computers may eventually help scientists:

Discover new medicines
Design stronger materials
Improve batteries
Study complicated molecules
Plan transportation routes
Develop cleaner energy
Understand diseases
Improve weather and climate research

Many of these possibilities are still being tested. Quantum computers are not yet powerful or reliable enough to perform most of these tasks better than ordinary computers.

Why Are Quantum Computers So Difficult to Build?

Qubits are extremely fragile.

Heat, vibration, electrical interference, and tiny manufacturing errors can disturb them. Some quantum computers must be kept at temperatures colder than outer space.

Scientists are working to reduce errors and keep quantum information stable long enough to complete useful calculations.

Quantum Sensors

Quantum sensors may become useful sooner than large quantum computers.

These sensors can make extremely precise measurements. They may help doctors see inside the body, help ships or aircraft navigate when GPS is unavailable, locate objects underground, monitor the environment, and improve scientific research.

Atomic clocks are an example of quantum technology already being used today. They help support GPS, communications, banking, and other important systems.

Quantum Communication

Quantum communication may provide new ways to protect sensitive information.

It can use the laws of quantum physics to reveal whether someone has attempted to intercept a message.

However, no communication system is completely secure. Computers, software, passwords, and human mistakes can still create weaknesses.

Could Quantum Computers Break Encryption?

A powerful future quantum computer may be able to break some of the encryption currently protecting websites, financial systems, government information, and private communications.

Present quantum computers cannot do this on a large scale.

Governments and technology companies are already developing new forms of encryption designed to withstand future quantum attacks. This is called post-quantum cryptography.

Is Quantum Technology Being Exaggerated?

There is genuine progress, although there is also a great deal of publicity and exaggeration.

Companies sometimes announce impressive numbers of qubits without clearly explaining how reliable those qubits are. A machine with many unstable qubits may be less useful than a smaller and more accurate machine.

Promises about instant medical cures, unbeatable artificial intelligence, or computers that solve every problem should be treated carefully.

Why Does This Matter?

Quantum technology could eventually improve medicine, strengthen cybersecurity, create better materials, support cleaner energy, and provide more accurate measuring tools.

It could also increase military competition, threaten older encryption, and give powerful countries or corporations greater technological control.

Quantum technology is not magic, and it is not ready to transform everyday life immediately. It is an important developing field that may gradually change science, security, medicine, and industry.

The wisest response is neither fear nor blind excitement. We should follow its progress carefully, prepare for its risks, and use its discoveries responsibly.

Quantum technology seeks to control some of the smallest and strangest features of nature and turn them into useful machines.

The field includes far more than futuristic computers. It also includes extraordinarily sensitive measuring instruments, highly accurate clocks, secure communication methods, and networks that may someday connect quantum devices across great distances.

The three most developed areas are quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum sensing. Each uses the laws of quantum mechanics differently, and each is progressing at a different speed.

What Makes Something Quantum?

Ordinary computers store and process information using bits. A bit represents either a zero or a one.

A quantum computer uses quantum bits, usually called qubits. Under controlled conditions, a qubit can exist in a combination of possible states known as superposition. Multiple qubits can also become entangled, meaning their properties are connected in ways that cannot be fully explained by treating each particle separately.

Quantum algorithms manipulate these combined possibilities through interference. Some possible answers are strengthened, while others are weakened or canceled. When the qubits are measured, however, the delicate quantum state collapses into an ordinary result.

This does not mean that a quantum computer simply tries every possible answer at once and instantly selects the correct one. That popular explanation is too simplistic. A useful quantum algorithm must be carefully designed so that interference makes the desired information more likely to appear when the system is measured.

The Problem of Quantum Fragility

Quantum states are extremely delicate.

Heat, vibration, electromagnetic interference, imperfect controls, microscopic manufacturing defects, and interactions with the surrounding environment can introduce errors. The loss of usable quantum behavior is known as decoherence.

Some quantum computers therefore operate inside elaborate refrigeration systems at temperatures close to absolute zero. Other designs use trapped ions, neutral atoms, photons, semiconductor devices, or other physical systems.

Each approach has advantages and weaknesses. Researchers have not yet established one universally superior form of quantum hardware.

The number of qubits also tells only part of the story. A machine containing many unreliable qubits may accomplish less than one containing fewer, higher-quality qubits. Important measurements include error rates, connectivity, operating speed, coherence time, and the complexity of circuits the machine can execute accurately.

Quantum Error Correction

A fully useful quantum computer will probably require quantum error correction.

Because unknown quantum information cannot simply be copied like an ordinary computer file, error correction must distribute information across groups of physical qubits. Together, these physical qubits form a more dependable logical qubit.

This process requires substantial hardware. Depending on the quality of the components and the error-correction method, one reliable logical qubit may require many physical qubits.

Progress in error correction is therefore often more meaningful than announcements based only on total qubit counts. The central challenge is not merely constructing a larger processor. It is making the entire system more reliable as it grows.

Quantum Computing

Quantum computers are specialized machines intended to perform certain calculations using quantum effects.

They are not expected to replace laptops, phones, conventional servers, or supercomputers. Most daily tasks—including word processing, video playback, email, accounting, web browsing, and database management—are handled more efficiently by ordinary computers.

The more realistic future is hybrid computing. Conventional processors will manage most of a workload, while a quantum processor may be assigned a narrow calculation for which it offers a genuine advantage.

IBM, for example, has published plans for closer integration between quantum systems and classical supercomputers, along with tools for monitoring and debugging hybrid workloads. These are company roadmaps rather than guarantees of when broad commercial advantage will arrive.

Promising Uses of Quantum Computers

Chemistry and medicine

Nature is quantum mechanical at the molecular level. Simulating molecules accurately becomes extremely difficult for classical computers as their complexity increases.

Future quantum computers may help scientists model chemical reactions, proteins, catalysts, and pharmaceutical compounds. This could support drug discovery, although quantum computers would not eliminate laboratory testing, clinical trials, safety reviews, or the biological complexity of disease.

Materials and energy

Quantum simulation may help researchers investigate improved batteries, superconductors, fertilizers, solar materials, industrial catalysts, and methods of capturing carbon.

Even modest improvements in catalysts could reduce the energy required by major industrial processes. The difficulty is reaching a scale and accuracy that outperform the best classical methods on genuinely valuable problems.

Optimization

Organizations face complicated scheduling, routing, manufacturing, investment, and supply-chain problems.

Quantum algorithms may eventually help with certain forms of optimization. However, many optimization claims remain experimental. Classical algorithms also continue to improve, so a quantum method must be compared with the strongest conventional alternative rather than an outdated one.

Scientific discovery

Quantum computers may become valuable research instruments for nuclear physics, high-energy physics, materials science, and other fields involving complex quantum systems.

The Department of Energy has identified quantum information science as relevant to materials research, environmental measurement, navigation, dark-matter searches, gravitational-wave detection, and other scientific challenges.

Artificial intelligence

Quantum computing and artificial intelligence may eventually complement one another. AI can help calibrate quantum devices, analyze experimental results, and search for improved control methods.

Quantum algorithms might someday accelerate selected machine-learning calculations. Claims that quantum computers will automatically make all AI dramatically more powerful remain unproven.

Quantum Communication

Quantum communication transfers or shares information using quantum states, often carried by photons.

One important concept is quantum key distribution. It can allow two parties to create encryption keys while detecting certain attempts to intercept the quantum transmission. Measurement disturbs quantum states, so eavesdropping can leave detectable evidence.

Quantum key distribution does not make an entire communication system invulnerable. Hardware, software, users, authentication systems, and network endpoints can still be attacked.

Researchers are also working toward quantum networks that could connect sensors, processors, memories, and communication devices. Entanglement might eventually be distributed across these networks, enabling capabilities that ordinary networks cannot reproduce. NIST is developing components, prototypes, and test beds for quantum networking.

A large-scale “quantum internet” remains a long-term research goal. It would not simply be a faster version of today’s internet. It would likely operate alongside classical networks and provide specialized functions.

Quantum Sensing

Quantum sensing may affect society sooner than large fault-tolerant quantum computing.

Quantum sensors use properties such as atomic energy levels, spin, interference, and entanglement to measure time, gravity, magnetic fields, acceleration, rotation, temperature, and other physical quantities with exceptional precision.

Some quantum-based instruments already exist. Atomic clocks support precise timekeeping, navigation, telecommunications, scientific measurement, and the operation of modern infrastructure.

Emerging quantum sensors may help locate underground structures, detect subtle magnetic signals, improve medical imaging, monitor environmental changes, measure movement without relying entirely on GPS, and conduct fundamental scientific experiments.

Quantum sensing often does not require the enormous error-corrected processors needed for universal quantum computing. This may allow useful sensing products to mature earlier.

Navigation Without GPS

Modern transportation, communications, finance, energy systems, and military operations depend heavily upon accurate timing and satellite navigation.

Quantum accelerometers, gyroscopes, clocks, and gravimeters could contribute to navigation systems that continue operating when GPS signals are blocked, jammed, unavailable, or unreliable.

Such systems would not necessarily make satellites unnecessary. They could provide an additional layer of resilience for aircraft, ships, emergency services, research vessels, infrastructure, and national defense.

The Encryption Challenge

A sufficiently powerful fault-tolerant quantum computer could threaten widely used public-key cryptographic systems.

These systems help protect websites, financial transactions, government communications, software updates, digital identities, and private records. The danger does not mean present quantum machines can suddenly break all modern encryption. They cannot.

The concern is partly long-term. An attacker could collect encrypted information today and retain it until future technology becomes capable of decrypting it. This is sometimes described as “harvest now, decrypt later.”

The principal defense is post-quantum cryptography: conventional mathematical encryption designed to resist attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

Moving to new cryptographic systems takes years because organizations must identify vulnerable systems, update software and hardware, test compatibility, replace long-lived equipment, and protect archived information. Preparation should therefore begin before a cryptographically dangerous quantum computer exists.

Quantum communication and post-quantum cryptography should not be confused. One uses quantum physical systems; the other generally runs on conventional computers.

Where the Field Stands in 2026

Quantum research has entered a period of large public and private investment.

In November 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy announced $625 million to renew five National Quantum Information Science Research Centers. IBM announced in June 2026 that it planned to invest more than $10 billion in quantum computing over five years. These commitments show strategic confidence in the field, although investment does not guarantee that every technical roadmap will be achieved on schedule.

Researchers are improving control systems, fabrication, modular architectures, networking, software, and error-correction techniques. Companies are also attempting to integrate quantum processors with classical high-performance computing.

At the same time, the field remains vulnerable to exaggerated announcements. Experimental demonstrations may solve artificial benchmark problems without producing immediate commercial value. Company claims can also face scientific dispute, as recent criticism of Microsoft’s topological-qubit research illustrates.

Responsible evaluation requires published evidence, independent replication, meaningful benchmarks, and comparison with the best available classical machines.

Major Obstacles

Noise and errors

Qubits lose information and accumulate errors rapidly. Useful computation may require far greater reliability than most present systems provide.

Scaling

A laboratory device containing a small number of controllable qubits is very different from an industrial system containing enough logical qubits for valuable fault-tolerant computation.

Manufacturing

Quantum hardware requires extraordinary precision. Tiny variations can affect the behavior of individual qubits and reduce production yield.

Cooling and infrastructure

Some architectures require complex cryogenic equipment, shielding, lasers, vacuum systems, control electronics, or specialized facilities.

Software

Quantum programming demands new algorithms, compilers, verification methods, and ways to divide work between quantum and classical processors.

Verification

A difficult question arises when a quantum computer produces a result that no classical computer can independently reproduce. Researchers need trustworthy methods to verify the calculation.

Cost

Quantum systems remain expensive to build, operate, maintain, and calibrate. Early access will probably be concentrated in governments, universities, cloud providers, and large companies.

Economic and Social Effects

Quantum technology may create demand for physicists, engineers, programmers, technicians, mathematicians, cybersecurity specialists, materials scientists, and manufacturing workers.

The field also requires people who can connect scientific research with medicine, chemistry, business, law, ethics, and public policy.

Not every worker will need a doctorate in quantum physics. A mature industry will also require conventional software development, electronics, refrigeration, photonics, fabrication, maintenance, education, sales, and regulatory expertise.

At the same time, unequal access could concentrate scientific and economic power among a small number of countries and companies. Public research, education, shared infrastructure, international standards, and responsible cloud access may help broaden participation.

Military and Geopolitical Concerns

Quantum technology has implications for secure communication, navigation, surveillance, sensing, codebreaking, submarine detection, and advanced materials.

This makes it strategically important to governments. It also increases the danger of secrecy, international rivalry, export restrictions, and an expensive technological arms race.

Quantum research has many peaceful uses, yet the same capability may have both civilian and military applications. Nations will need to protect legitimate security interests while maintaining enough scientific cooperation to support safety, verification, and responsible standards.

Quantum technology raises several important questions:

Who should control the most powerful systems?

How should governments prevent misuse without blocking beneficial research?

How should claims of “quantum advantage” be tested?

How can society avoid wasting public money on hype?

How should privacy be protected during the transition to quantum-resistant security?

How can smaller nations, universities, and businesses participate without becoming permanently dependent on a few providers?

These questions should be addressed while the technology is developing, rather than after its effects become difficult to reverse.

What Quantum Technology Will Not Do

Quantum technology will not make every computer program faster.

It will not instantly cure disease, predict the future, eliminate uncertainty, make artificial intelligence all-knowing, or solve every mathematical problem.

Quantum entanglement does not permit ordinary information to travel faster than light.

Quantum computers also do not test every possibility and effortlessly reveal the correct answer. Their advantages depend upon particular algorithms, suitable hardware, low error rates, and problems whose structure can be exploited quantum mechanically.

Any claim that ignores these limitations should be treated cautiously.

Quantum computing may progress through increasingly capable hybrid systems. The decisive milestone will not be a dramatic qubit announcement. It will be the repeated solution of useful problems more accurately, quickly, or economically than the best classical alternatives.

New PhotonCap field research: TSMC Is Ahead in CPO. Samsung Is Putting a Third Chip Next to HBM.

This one started a year ago, in an advanced packaging session at a Samsung forum. Last week at Nano Korea, the same executive disclosed 2.xD, a package binding HBM, logic, and silicon photonics, and the deck I had photographed in person at OECC 2026 in Busan suddenly had its frame.

The verdict on today's race is honest: TSMC won switch CPO, with Broadcom's 102.4T switch sampling on COUPE engines and NVIDIA's photonics switches in production. The piece argues the next race is different. On the XPU-HBM interposer, three kinds of chips have to land together, and only Samsung makes all three: HBM already fused to its foundry through the SF4 base die, and a SiPh platform that now carries a 40+ device PDK, an IMEC-measured 224G MZM, and a first optical-module customer.

We also carry the counterevidence at full weight: the multi-die yield penalty, CoWoS integration of external HBM, and SK hynix verticalizing packaging in Indiana and Cheongju. Everything folds into one falsifiable yardstick: a named design win binding HBM, logic, and optical I/O within twelve months. 24 references, scenarios, and a July 30 earnings checklist inside.

text:
Goldman Sachs April 2026 report projects Silicon Photonics growing from 6 percent of the datacom market today to 45 percent by 2028.

Most people read that as a threat to laser companies.
It is actually the opposite for $SIVE.

Silicon is great at moving light around. But it cannot create light. That is just physics. You always need an external laser feeding light into a Silicon Photonics chip.
That external laser is what $SIVE makes.

As Silicon Photonics grows from 6 to 45 percent of the market the demand for external CW lasers grows right alongside it.

Goldman basically proved the opposite of what most people assume. More Silicon Photonics means more laser demand. Not less.

text:
BofA: Soitec 

> Upside Already Priced In: BofA believes Soitec's Photonics-related upside is fully valued by the market. Consensus expectations imply a high growth rate that already prices in acceleration from Near/Co-Packaged Optics (NPO/CPO), which remains highly uncertain.

> Growth Projections: BofA models a 50% revenue CAGR for Photonics-SOI between FY26–FY29E based on total die area forecasts, which is more conservative than consensus expectations.

> Market Risk: GlobalWafers' planned competitive expansion into Photonics and RF-SOI introduces un-derisked market share pressure for Soitec.

> Slow Recovery: Mobile represented over half of Soitec's FY26 revenue. Price hikes by smartphone manufacturers are creating downside risks to consensus projections.

> Inventory Drag: RF-SOI revenue remains heavily impacted by customer inventory digestion (sitting at roughly -2 million wafers), with a sluggish recovery due to weak end-market demand. BofA models an 11% segment revenue decline for FY27E before a gradual recovery in FY28/29E.

> Forecast Revisions: BofA cut its overall financial projections across the board due to weaker expectations in Mobile and Auto/Industrial segments, despite slight upward revisions in Edge AI:

text:
I’ve been getting into nano photonics lately and my mind has been having a good time with it.

I like subjects that make me feel smart for about ten minutes and then immediately humble me again lol.

Keeps things balanced.

BTW, the screen turning white is not a glitch but done on purpose.

text:
光通信板块为什么跌这么惨？拆解Rosenblatt最新报告，聊聊我自己的判断

市场的担忧从哪来？
主要是三个因素叠加在一起：
一是市场担心中国厂商正在快速扩产磷化铟(InP)激光器产能， 害怕未来供给暴增导致价格战。
二是市场传出CPO(共封装光学)可能要推迟大约一年才能大规模部署， 这让不少人开始怀疑未来几年的光通信需求会不会跟着延后。
三是不少空头其实并不是真的看空这条赛道的基本面， 只是在借着这些消息和财报前的空窗期制造恐慌， 甚至有相当一部分空头计划在7月底、8月初财报公布前陆续回补空单， 部分人还打算2027年重新做多整个板块， 提前布局2028到2030年的CPO超级周期。

中国扩产到底是不是真正的威胁？
这是市场最关心的问题， 但我认为担心得有点过头了。 第一， 即便中国厂商持续扩产， 预计到2030年全球InP激光器的供给相比需求仍然存在大约40%的缺口， 行业整体依然缺货， 扩产短期内不会导致供给过剩。 
第二， 中国目前主要能量产的是低功率连续波激光器和100G EML， 应用场景集中在硅光收发器和800G模块这些相对成熟的产品线， 但真正未来几年最赚钱的200G EML和高功率连续波激光器， 中国还没有成熟的量产能力， 而这两样恰恰是CPO和NPO最核心的光源器件， 这意味着未来几年欧美厂商在高端环节依然握有明显的技术优势。 
第三， 中美供应链本身也在逐步脱钩， 出于政治因素、供应链安全、技术路线差异和客户认证体系不同这些原因， 欧美客户大概率不会转而采购中国厂商的产品， 目前Lumentum和Coherent很多产品已经排产到2027甚至2028年， 其中不少订单来自英伟达的提前锁单。

CPO到底有没有真的延期？
我倾向于认为没有实质性延期。 
CPO大致可以分两个阶段看：
第一阶段是小规模建设， 2026年下半年开始动工、2027年开始出货， 目前进度仍然按计划推进， 预计英伟达和博通2027年各自的产量都在3万到35万套左右。
第二阶段才是真正的大规模放量， 时间点在2028年。 而2027年这一年， NPO(近封装光学)会作为中间过渡方案承接一部分需求。

网络架构变化会不会减少光模块的需求？
市场还担心一件事，像MRC、RNG这类新的网络技术出现后， 会不会减少每台交换机需要的光模块数量。 确实有可能会从原来的6到7个下降到3到4个， 但GPU数量的增长速度远快于这个下降幅度， 整体光学需求预计仍然会持续增长， 这一点担心也偏多余。

几家公司的财报看点
Lumentum这次财报压力相对最大， 因为它此前已经给出了市场上最激进的长期指引， 市场关注的是它能不能进一步上调2027年目标， 尤其是单季营收能不能达到20亿美元 (年化80亿美元)、营业利润率能不能超过40%。 Coherent这次是市场机构里比较偏爱的一家， 因为200G EML和高功率激光器的自产比例在持续提升， 加上刚拿到谷歌每年约10亿美元的1.6T模块订单， 以及英伟达2027到2030年的长期采购协议， 未来利润率有望持续改善。 
AAOI这次财报可能会是营收和指引双双超预期的一份成绩单， 主要动力来自亚马逊和甲骨文的800G订单， 预计二季度收入能超过2亿美元、三季度指引接近3亿美元， 毛利率也会明显高于上半年大约30%的水平。 ANET的增长动力依然强劲， 主要客户覆盖Meta、微软、甲骨文、OpenAI、Anthropic、谷歌， 其中谷歌未来有可能成为它超过10%收入占比的大客户， 大量递延收入预计从2026年下半年开始确认， 带动下半年收入增速超过30%、2027年超过40%。

这份报告的核心结论
总结下来， 中国扩产确实是真实存在的现象， 但短期内不足以改变全球的供需格局。真正具备技术壁垒的200G EML、高功率激光器以及CPO相关产品， 欧美厂商依然领先。2028年仍然是CPO真正大规模放量的时间点， 2027年由NPO承接过渡需求。
目前光通信板块的这轮回调更多是情绪和预期驱动， 基本面没有恶化。即将到来的7月底到8月初财报季， 会是验证这条赛道需求是否依然强劲的重要窗口。

看完这份报告，我想补充一个自己的判断
我认为，短期缺货和未来出现结构性过剩并不矛盾（2026 2027年非常看好，2028年以后要注意），这两件事可以同时成立，关键要看过剩最先会出现在哪个环节。

现在整个行业都看到了AI需求，也都在积极扩产，云厂商也希望引入更多供应商来降低成本、分散供应链风险。如果未来800G、1.6T模块供应商越来越多，ASP下降速度开始快于成本下降速度，那么即使出货量和收入继续增长，毛利率也可能率先承压。

不过从目前来看，行业还远没有走到这一步。各家公司披露的信息依然指向供给紧张：高端激光器排产排到未来几年，积压订单维持高位，出口限制、良率、客户认证和测试能力仍然是主要瓶颈，真正缺的还是合格的高端产能。

如果未来行业进入供需再平衡，我认为最先面临价格压力的，大概率还是标准化程度较高的光模块环节，例如400G、逐渐成熟的800G，以及未来进入主流阶段的1.6T产品。像AAOI、中际旭创、新易盛、Source Photonics这类以光模块为主的厂商，未来更需要关注ASP和毛利率的变化。不过这是2028年以后更值得关注的风险，至少在我看来，2026至2027年行业仍然处于供不应求阶段，AAOI等公司依然受益于订单释放、产能爬坡和业绩兑现。

相比之下，我更看好拥有核心器件壁垒的高端零部件公司，例如Lumentum（LITE）、Coherent（COHR）；以及Ciena（CIEN）、Nokia（NOK）等系统层公司，它们更多依靠技术、网络架构和系统集成能力竞争。至于Veeco（VECO）、Aehr（AEHR）、Viavi（VIAV）等设备和测试公司，只要行业仍在扩产，就有望持续受益于资本开支周期。

时间线上，我的判断和这份报告基本一致：2026年至2027年上半年仍是供不应求；2027年下半年至2028年，随着新增产能逐步释放，市场会开始重新定价供需关系。

现在讨论整个光通信行业全面产能过剩还为时过早。未来几年，不同产品、不同技术路线和不同公司，大概率会进入完全不同的供需周期，不会整个行业一起过剩。

1735, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 1753}]}, "text": "光通信板块为什么跌这么惨？拆解Rosenblatt最新报告，聊聊我自己的判断 市场的担忧从哪来？ 主要是三个因素叠加在一起： 一是市场担心中国厂商正在快速扩产磷化铟(InP)激光器产能， 害怕未来供给暴增导致价格战。 二是市场传出CPO(共封装光学)可能要推迟大约一年才能大规模部署， 这让不少人开始怀疑未来几年的光通信需求会不会跟着延后。 三是不少空头其实并不是真的看空这条赛道的基本面， 只是在借着这些消息和财报前的空窗期制造恐慌， 甚至有相当一部分空头计划在7月底、8月初财报公布前陆续回补空单， 部分人还打算2027年重新做多整个板块， 提前布局2028到2030年的CPO超级周期。 中国扩产到底是不是真正的威胁？ 这是市场最关心的问题， 但我认为担心得有点过头了。 第一， 即便中国厂商持续扩产， 预计到2030年全球InP激光器的供给相比需求仍然存在大约40%的缺口， 行业整体依然缺货， 扩产短期内不会导致供给过剩。 第二， 中国目前主要能量产的是低功率连续波激光器和100G EML， 应用场景集中在硅光收发器和800G模块这些相对成熟的产品线， 但真正未来几年最赚钱的200G EML和高功率连续波激光器， 中国还没有成熟的量产能力， 而这两样恰恰是CPO和NPO最核心的光源器件， 这意味着未来几年欧美厂商在高端环节依然握有明显的技术优势。 第三， 中美供应链本身也在逐步脱钩， 出于政治因素、供应链安全、技术路线差异和客户认证体系不同这些原因， 欧美客户大概率不会转而采购中国厂商的产品， 目前Lumentum和Coherent很多产品已经排产到2027甚至2028年， 其中不少订单来自英伟达的提前锁单。 CPO到底有没有真的延期？ 我倾向于认为没有实质性延期。 CPO大致可以分两个阶段看： 第一阶段是小规模建设， 2026年下半年开始动工、2027年开始出货， 目前进度仍然按计划推进， 预计英伟达和博通2027年各自的产量都在3万到35万套左右。 第二阶段才是真正的大规模放量， 时间点在2028年。 而2027年这一年， NPO(近封装光学)会作为中间过渡方案承接一部分需求。 网络架构变化会不会减少光模块的需求？ 市场还担心一件事，像MRC、RNG这类新的网络技术出现后， 会不会减少每台交换机需要的光模块数量。 确实有可能会从原来的6到7个下降到3到4个， 但GPU数量的增长速度远快于这个下降幅度， 整体光学需求预计仍然会持续增长， 这一点担心也偏多余。 几家公司的财报看点 Lumentum这次财报压力相对最大， 因为它此前已经给出了市场上最激进的长期指引， 市场关注的是它能不能进一步上调2027年目标， 尤其是单季营收能不能达到20亿美元 (年化80亿美元)、营业利润率能不能超过40%。 Coherent这次是市场机构里比较偏爱的一家， 因为200G EML和高功率激光器的自产比例在持续提升， 加上刚拿到谷歌每年约10亿美元的1.6T模块订单， 以及英伟达2027到2030年的长期采购协议， 未来利润率有望持续改善。 AAOI这次财报可能会是营收和指引双双超预期的一份成绩单， 主要动力来自亚马逊和甲骨文的800G订单， 预计二季度收入能超过2亿美元、三季度指引接近3亿美元， 毛利率也会明显高于上半年大约30%的水平。 ANET的增长动力依然强劲， 主要客户覆盖Meta、微软、甲骨文、OpenAI、Anthropic、谷歌， 其中谷歌未来有可能成为它超过10%收入占比的大客户， 大量递延收入预计从2026年下半年开始确认， 带动下半年收入增速超过30%、2027年超过40%。 这份报告的核心结论 总结下来， 中国扩产确实是真实存在的现象， 但短期内不足以改变全球的供需格局。真正具备技术壁垒的200G EML、高功率激光器以及CPO相关产品， 欧美厂商依然领先。2028年仍然是CPO真正大规模放量的时间点， text:
James Reilly read about arthritis biomarkers and photonics back to back, saw the overlap, and built a search engine for it.

Our cover story on @latentknwledge

https://t.co/hH8urwuxmi via Yespress

If you want to win in this market, you have to ask yourself: what is the crowd panic-selling right now purely based on price, while the physical layer fundamentals are actually getting stronger?

When I look across Photonics, Semis, and Advanced Materials right now, a few clear names stand out:

$SIVE: Crashed 55% from the highs on pure retail panic, completely ignoring that institutional lenders just swapped $12M in debt for equity at the absolute bottom. The Indium Phosphide (InP) cleanroom expansions and Nasdaq spin-off mean the structural AI data center moat is fully intact.

$AAOI: Smashed by a temporary macro liquidity drain as institutional desks reallocated capital to absorb massive semi listings. Meanwhile, the back-half revenue inflection is entirely unchanged, and the upcoming Q3 guide is the only thing that actually matters.

$POET / $LWLG: The crowd treats next-gen optical engines like a waiting game, completely missing the absolute physical necessity of getting hardware power consumption down as backend clusters scale to 800G and 1.6T.

When retail sees red candles, they lose their minds and entirely miss the actual thesis of the order book.

What other forgotten physical layer or semi favorites are you guys monitoring down here with the recent drop in prices ? 📈👇

text:
Great breakdown

What makes this space so interesting is that the winners may not be the companies selling the final systems, but the suppliers sitting at the hardest bottlenecks across the stack.

For me, the most asymmetric areas remain:

• Photonics: $SIVE $AAOI
• InP substrates: $AXTI $IQE
• Connectivity silicon: $CRDO $MRVL
• Test equipment: $AEHR $TRT

The real question is which layer sees demand accelerate faster than capacity can respond.
