Kapsamlı Şirket Değerleme ve Stratejik Analiz 

1. Yönetici Özeti ve Stratejik Yol Ayrımı

Yarı iletken endüstrisinde Moore Kanunu'nun fiziksel sınırlara dayanması ve ön uç (front-end) litografi maliyetlerinin katlanarak artması, performans artışının yeni odağını İleri Seviye Paketleme  ve Heterojen Entegrrasyon süreçlerine kaydırmıştır. Kulicke & Soffa, tarihsel olarak otomotiv, endüstriyel ve tüketici elektroniğine hitap eden olgun tel bağlama pazarında %80’e yakın pazar payıyla ezici bir cash cow yönetmektedir. Ancak şirket, son iki yıldır akademik ve kurumsal analizlere konu olan (örneğin 2025 sonundaki Harvard Business School stratejik vaka analizindeki gibi) çok kritik bir yol ayrımındadır: Olgun pazarlardaki nakit akışını koruyarak, yapay zeka (AI) ve yüksek performanslı bilgi işlem (HPC) çiplerinin mimarisini belirleyen ileri seviye montaj teknolojilerine liderlik etmek.

FY2026 ikinci çeyrek sonuçları ve güncel pazar dinamikleri, KLIC’in bu teknolojik dönüşümü başarıyla gerçekleştirdiğini ve döngüsel bir dipten çıkış (cyclical inflection) dönemine girdiğini doğrulamaktadır. Şirket, karlılığı düşük olan Electronics Assembly iş kolunu tamamen kapatarak odağını netleştirmiş, yapay zeka veri merkezlerindeki bant genişliği kısıtlamalarını çözen Thermo-Compression Bonding ve Silicon Photonics çözümlerinde vites artırmıştır.

2. Finansal Performans ve Döngüsel Kaldıraç Analizi

KLIC’in finansal yapısı, arka uç (back-end) sermaye ekipmanı üreticilerinde görülen yüksek operasyonel kaldıraç karakteristiğini taşımaktadır. Sektörel envanter düzeltmelerinin sona ermesiyle birlikte, gelir artışı marjlar üzerinde çarpan etkisi yaratmaktadır.

Çeyreklik Finansal Gerçekleşmeler ve Dinamikler

-- Gelir İvmesi: Q2 FY2026 net geliri, bir önceki çeyreğe göre %21.5, geçen yılın aynı dönemine göre ise %49.8 artarak 242.6 milyon USD seviyesine ulaşmıştır. Bu rakam, konsensüs beklentisi olan 191.4 milyon USD’yi büyük bir sürprizle geride bırakmıştır.

-- Brüt Kar Marjı Genişlemesi: Brüt marj %49.3 olarak gerçekleşmiştir. Geçen yılın aynı çeyreğindeki %24.9'luk seviyeye kıyasla 2,440 baz puanlık operasyonel genişleme, yüksek marjlı Advanced Solutions ve bellek ürün miksinin devreye girmesiyle açıklanmaktadır.

-- Karlılığa Dönüş: GAAP operasyonel karı 38.5 milyon USD'ye yükselmiştir. Geçen yılın aynı döneminde kaydedilen ağır zararların ardından, Non-GAAP operasyonel kar marjı %19.1’e (46.3 milyon USD) fırlamıştır. Non-GAAP EPS ise 0.79 USD ile beklentilerin 0.12 USD üzerinde gelmiştir.

Q3 ve Q4 FY2026 Öngörüleri

Şirketin resmi Q3 FY2026 kılavuzu, ardışık olarak %28 büyüme ile 310 milyon USD gelir hedeflemektedir. Operasyonel giderlerin (OpEx) 85 milyon USD seviyesine çıkması, üretim hacmini (production ramp) desteklemek için yapılan mühendislik istihdamları ve değişken performans primlerinden kaynaklanmaktadır. Yönetim, Q4 FY2026 döneminde de Q3'e kıyasla %5 ile %10 arasında organik bir büyüme trendinin korunacağını öngörmektedir. Bu doğrultuda FY2026 toplam geliri yaklaşık 1.07 milyar USD bandına doğru ilerlemektedir.

3. Segment Bazında Operasyonel ve Teknolojik Derinleşme

KLIC’in ürün portföyü, yarı iletken dünyasının hem olgun hem de en uç teknolojilerine eş zamanlı hizmet verecek şekilde yeniden yapılandırılmıştır.

-- Olgun Pazarlar: Ball/Wedge Bonding ──> Nakit Üretimi & Hacim (Akıllı Telefon, Otomotiv, Endüstriyel)

-- Seküler Pazarlar: Advanced Solutions ──> TCB, FTC, ProMEM Suite, Vertical Wire (AI, HPC, HBM)

-- İstikrar Segmenti: APS ──> Fabrika Kullanım Oranlarına Bağlı Yedek Parça ve Servis Güvencesi

A. Geleneksel ve Hafif Döngüsel Segmentler

-- Ball Bonding Equipment (Tarihsel Pay: %44.8): Klasik altın ve bakır tel bağlama sistemleridir. Akıllı telefon, PC ve alt segment tüketici elektroniğindeki döngüsel toparlanma, bu segmenti Q2 FY2026'da yeniden ana gelir sürücülerinden biri haline getirmiştir (GenSemi gelirleri çeyreklik %19.4 artışla 148.9 milyon USD olmuştur).

-- Wedge Bonding Equipment (Tarihsel Pay: %16.9): Güç yarı iletkenleri, elektrikli araç (EV) batarya paketleri ve endüstriyel modüllere hizmet eder. Otomotiv sektöründeki elektrifikasyon trendiyle daha stabil bir grafik çizmektedir.

-- Aftermarket Products & Services (APS) (Tarihsel Pay: %23.9): Küresel kurulu makine parkının sarf malzemeleri ve servis hizmetleridir. Donanım satışları düşse dahi, fabrikaların çalışmasına paralel olarak sürekli nakit üreten, döngüselliği en düşük defansif kalkandır.

B. Yeni Nesil Hafıza (Memory) ve Güç Çözümleri (2026 İnovasyonları)

Bellek segmenti, kurumsal SSD (eSSD) ve yüksek performanslı sunucu talebiyle Q2 FY2026'da çeyreklik bazda %93 büyüme kaydederek 31.3 milyon USD'ye ulaşmıştır. Şirket bu ivmeyi kalıcı kılmak adına Mart 2026'da iki büyük teknolojik silahını duyurmuştur:

1. ProMEM Suite: Standart yüksek hacimli bellek montajında (Stacked NAND) üretkenliği ve proses kontrolünü dramatik şekilde artıran bir ball bonding yazılım ve donanım paketidir.

2. Vertical Wire Teknolojisi (ATP MEM PLUS): Geleneksel tel bağlamayı dikey boyuta taşıyarak ara bağlantı yoğunluğunu artırır ve paket ayak izini küçültür. Stacked DRAM ve yeni nesil yüksek yoğunluklu bellek mimarilerinde silikon delikli geçişlere (TSV) karşı son derece maliyet etkin bir alternatif sunmaktadır.

4. Gelişmiş Paketleme ve AI Altyapı Katalizörü

Advanced Solutions segmenti altındaki Thermo-Compression Bonding (TCB), KLIC'in uzun vadeli seküler büyüme motorudur. Şirket, TCB iş kolunun FY2026’da çeyreklik bazda en az %70 büyüyeceğini ve tek başına 100 milyon USD+ barajını aşacağını doğrulamıştır.

Fluxless TCB (FTC) ve Teknik Üstünlük

Gelişmiş veri merkezi işlemcilerinde kullanılan alt bileşenlerin kalınlıkları azaldıkça ve kalıp boyutları büyüdükçe (30 mm ve üzeri), geleneksel "flip-chip" yöntemleri termal eğrilme ve hizalama hatalarına neden olmaktadır. KLIC’in Fluxless TCB (FTC) teknolojisi bu problemi şu inovasyonlarla çözmektedir:

-- Mikron Altı Hassasiyet: Lehim tabanlı bağlantıları 10 μm hatveye (pitch), doğrudan bakır-bakır bağlantıları ise en az 5 μm hatveye kadar indirebilmektedir.

-- Formik Asit Buharı Oksit İndirgeme: Süreç esnasında sıvı akı (flux) kullanılmadığı için kalıplar arasında neredeyse "sıfır boşluk yüksekliği" elde edilir; bu da elektriksel direnci düşürür ve termal yönetimi optimize eder.

-- Co-Packaged Optics ve Silikon Fotonik: Yapay zeka kümelerindeki anahtarlama ve ağ hızlarını artırmak için optik transceiver bileşenlerinin doğrudan işlemci yanına TCB ile montajlanması sürecinde KLIC, küresel lider dökümhanelerden ve OSAT'lardan peş peşe siparişler almaktadır.

5. Kapasite Genişletme ve Gelecek Projeksiyonları

Advanced Solutions segmentinin ölçeklenebilmesi için üretim darboğazlarının aşılması gerekmektedir. Şirket yönetimi bu doğrultuda stratejik bir sermaye hamlesi yapmıştır:

1. CapEx Artırımı
CapEx Artırımı:FY2026 Dönemi.Şirket, yıllık sermaye harcaması (CapEx) bütçesini 12 milyon USD seviyesinden 22 milyon USD seviyesine neredeyse iki katına çıkarmıştır.

2. Üretim Hatlarının Modernizasyonu:
Q3/Q4 FY2026. Artırılan bütçe doğrudan Singapur ve ilgili üretim tesislerinde Fluxless TCB ve dikey tel montaj hatlarının otomasyon kalitesini yükseltmek için kullanılmaktadır.

3.Kapasite Hedefine Ulaşılması:
Erken FY2027. Yapılan bu yatırımlarla Advanced Solutions segmentinin yıllık üretim ve sevkiyat kapasitesi run-rate bazında 400 milyon USD satış potansiyeline ulaştırılacaktır.

Sipariş birikimini gösteren backlog yapısı da bu süreci desteklemektedir ve hızla artmakdadır. Siparişlerin iptal edilebilir olması bir risk olsa da Çin, Tayvan ve Kore'deki fabrika kullanım oranlarının %90'ın üzerinde seyretmesi, bu siparişlerin büyük ölçüde realizasyonla sonuçlanacağını göstermektedir.

6. Sektörel Karşılaştırma ve İleri Dönük Değerleme Modeli

Yarı iletken ekipman pazarında değerleme yapılırken ön uç (ASML, KLA, Lam Research, Applied Materials) ve arka uç (KLIC, Besi, ASMPT) ayrımı kritik rol oynar. Ön uç firmaları daha az döngüsel ve yüksek bariyerli oldukları için tarihsel olarak yüksek çarpanlara sahiptir.

KLIC ise tarihsel olarak olgun wire bonder pazarının dalgalanmalarına maruz kaldığı için düşük çarpanla işlem görmüştür. Fakat Advanced Solutions segmentinin kapasitesinin 400 milyon USD'ye ayarlanması ve TCB'nin yüksek karlılığı, çarpan genişlemesini tetiklemektedir.

Akran Grubu Karşılaştırma Analizi (FOTO - 1)

2027 Gelir ve Çarpan Projeksiyonu

-- FY2026 Tahmini Gelir: ~1.07 milyar USD (Mevcut çeyreklik gidişat ve Q3 kılavuzu tabanlı).

-- FY2027 Projeksiyon Geliri: Bellek toparlanması, ProMEM Suite adaptasyonu ve TCB'nin yıllık 100 milyon USD'lik barajı kırıp Advanced Solutions run-rate'ini yukarı taşımasıyla 1.25 milyar USD (%17-20 civarı bir yıllık büyüme).

-- Değerleme Yaklaşımı: Advanced Packaging payının artması sebebiyle şirkete hak ettiği tarihsel iskontonun üzerinde, 6x Forward P/S (İleri Dönük Satış) çarpanı uygulanması rasyoneldir. Bu çarpan altında şirketin adil kurumsal değeri 7.5 milyar USD olarak hesaplanmaktadır.

Şirketin kasasındaki 254.3 milyon USD net nakit ve sıfır borç yapısı, hisse başına değer hesaplamasında ekstra bir emniyet marjı sunmaktadır. Finansal modelleme kapsamında %8 iskonto oranı tatbik edildiğinde, hisse senedinin önümüzdeki 12 aylık periyotta 135 USD adil değer hedefine doğru re-rate edilmesi beklenmektedir. Bu da mevcut konsolide fiyata göre yaklaşık %20 yukarı potansiyel anlamına gelir.

7. Derinlemesine Risk ve Tehdit Analizi

Risk Matrisi ve Stratejik Tehditler: KLIC pozisyonlanmasında aşağı yönlü risklerin yakından izlenmesi gerekmektedir.

I. İkame Teknolojiler ve Hibrit Bağlama Tehdidi

En uç seviye AI hızlandırıcılarında (NVIDIA, AMD vb. yeni nesil mimarileri) TCB hatvelerinin dahi yetersiz kaldığı noktalarda Hybrid Bonding (Doğrudan Bakır-Bakır Die-to-Wafer entegrasyonu) devreye girmektedir. Bu alanda Hollandalı BE Semiconductor (Besi) tartışmasız pazar lideridir ve ana dökümhanelerin (TSMC vb.) onaylı tedarikçisidir.

Risk: Eğer Hybrid Bonding teknolojisi beklenenden daha hızlı ucuzlar ve orta segment çiplere de hızla adapte edilirse, KLIC'in TCB pazarı daralabilir. KLIC, 2024'ten bu yana kendi bünyesindeki Hibrit Bağlama AR-GE programını hızlandırdığını ve erken müşteri test aşamasında olduğunu (Mart 2026 güncellemelerinde) belirtse de Besi ile olan teknolojik ve zamansal makası kapatamama riski mevcuttur.

II. Müşteri Konsantrasyonu ve Kamçı Etkisi
KLIC’in gelir yapısı son derece dikeydir. En büyük 10 müşteri (büyük OSAT'lar olan ASE, Amkor ve dev IDM'ler) toplam grup gelirlerinin %55'ini oluşturmaktadır.

Risk: Son tüketici elektroniğindeki ani bir makroekonomik durgunluk, bu 10 dev firmanın CapEx bütçelerini anında dondurmasına yol açar. Arka uç üreticileri, tedarik zincirinin en sonunda yer aldığından "Kamçı Etkisi" nedeniyle sipariş iptallerini veya ertelemelerini en şiddetli hisseden grup olmaktadır.

III. Jeopolitik Sıkışma ve Doğu Asya Riski

KLIC her ne kadar Singapur merkezli ve ABD operasyonlu bir yapıya sahip olsa da en büyük yarı iletken montaj müşterileri ve dökümhaneleri Tayvan, Çin Halk Cumhuriyeti ve Güney Kore sınırları içerisindedir.

Risk: ABD-Çin ticaret savaşlarının gelişmiş paketleme ekipmanlarına (özellikle TCB ve alt bileşenlerine) yönelik getirebileceği ek ihracat yasakları veya Tayvan Boğazı'ndaki olası bir lojistik aksama, şirketin backlog teslimatlarını tamamen felç edebilir.

IV. CapEx ve Marj Baskısı

Yıllık CapEx'in 12 milyon USD'den 22 milyon USD'ye çıkarılması, kısa vadede nakit akışını ve serbest nakit akışı marjlarını (Q2 FY2026'da düzeltilmiş serbest nakit akışı 6.3 milyon USD seviyesindedir) baskılamaktadır. Yeni hatların verimlilik oranlarında yaşanabilecek herhangi bir operasyonel aksaklık, Q3 ve Q4 brüt marj tahminlerini aşağı yönlü revize ettirebilir.

8. Stratejik Sonuç

Kulicke & Soffa, döngüsel bir çöküşün ardından operasyonel karlılığa dönüş hikayesini başarıyla yazmıştır. Şirket, geleneksel iş kollarının sağladığı güçlü nakit akışını, geleceğin AI ve bellek standartları olan TCB, ProMEM ve dikey tel teknolojilerine başarıyla aktarmaktadır. Hisse senedinde son aylarda yaşanan güçlü momentum ve ralli, bu döngüsel dönüşün pazar tarafından satın alınmaya başladığını gösterse de FY2027 projeksiyonlarımız doğrultusunda şirketin çarpan büyümesinin henüz tamamlanmadığı değerlendirilmektedir. Bilançosunda borç bulunmayan, düzenli çeyreklik temettü ödeyen ve hisse geri alımlarıyla paydaş değerini destekleyen KLIC, uzun vadeli yarı iletken portföylerinde temel bir arka uç pozisyonu olarak öne çıkmaktadır.

text:
Sometimes I forget how rare investors like us are. 

I know many people in my country aren't even investing and have all their wealth on a savings account.

Then you have people who do invest, but only in indices. They add money occasionally and for the rest they never look back.

Then you have people who invest in stocks, but only the safe ones or dividend stocks, like $KO, $MCD, $OXY, etc.

And then you have us. Investors in AI, memory, photonics, space, you name it, with a very high amount of conviction. I've seen my portfolio go up or down over 10% on a single day several times this year and I barely flinch at this point.

We are a rare species.

text:
$BURU Not financial advice. Not too often a penny stock catches my eye. the pivot from blue lasers to infusing those blue laser capabilities into counter UAS, drones, defense, software, photonics all under one roof to go along with their NATO ties for pennies on the dollar is quite fascinating. 

A lot of risks with the financial health that's true but upside could be worth it at these levels (currently $0.1481 per share ) Loaded 2100 shares yesterday at $0.1417. The more I research the more I'm thinking I should've bought more. Probably 10x my share count these next couple weeks.  

A lot of regret not catching $ONDS earlier (entered at $8). I personally like the defense sector as a whole at these low valuations, but $BURU seems like quite the sleeper.

Management is outlining a more than $300 million development plan to build an integrated defense and advanced technology platform, shifting the company away from being known primarily as a laser technology business. Think of it as a complete business transformation. Instead of relying mainly on industrial laser products, Nuburu wants to combine defense electronics, secure communications, AI, cybersecurity, drones, advanced manufacturing, and military technologies under one organization. If executed successfully, the company could evolve into a national defense technology platform rather than a niche hardware supplier. The timing is also important. Europe is rapidly increasing defense spending as NATO members commit to higher military budgets. Governments are looking to strengthen domestic defense supply chains instead of relying heavily on foreign suppliers. Tekne already has relationships in Italy’s defense sector, giving Nuburu an entry point into this growing market. Think about companies like $PLTR, $KTOS, $LHX, $LMT, $NOC, and $RTX. These businesses have generated strong long-term growth by providing software, autonomous systems, communications, sensors, and defense technologies. Nuburu appears to be positioning itself to participate in a similar ecosystem, although at a much earlier stage. The opportunity is significant because defense contracts are typically multi-year, recurring, and government-backed, providing greater revenue visibility than many commercial markets. However, investors should also recognize the risks. A turnaround of this size requires integrating acquisitions, securing new contracts, raising capital when needed, and successfully executing a completely new strategy. Until the company begins winning meaningful defense contracts and generating sustainable revenue, much of today’s valuation will depend on investor confidence in management’s execution. Bottom line: Investors are becoming interested because Nuburu is no longer being viewed solely as a laser company. If management successfully transforms it into an integrated European defense and advanced technology platform, the company’s long-term opportunity could be substantially larger than its legacy business. The next major catalysts will be contract wins, acquisition integration, government partnerships, and evidence that the $300 million development plan is translating into real revenue growth."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2074769379749748791"}}, "rest_id": "2074783189860753420", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2307", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2076006116652102041", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1397", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$VECO — A semiconductor equipment company positioned at the intersection of AI, HBM, and advanced chips. Ideal entry zone $30-45.

• Manufactures critical semiconductor process equipment used for HBM/DRAM memory, advanced packaging, compound semiconductors, photonics, and AI-related chips.

• FY2025 revenue reached $664M, with semiconductor revenue representing 72% of total sales.

• AI-driven demand is accelerating opportunities in HBM memory, advanced packaging, and high-performance computing as chip complexity increases.

• Secured momentum in advanced semiconductor markets, including laser annealing systems for leading-edge logic and memory customers and continued adoption of new technologies.

• Trading at a much lower valuation than many AI semiconductor names, while providing exposure to the “picks and shovels” layer of the AI buildout.

AI needs more than GPUs.

It needs the equipment that builds the memory, packaging, and chips powering the next generation of compute.

text:
**July 9-10, 2026 | CPO & Photonics Daily Update**

CPO momentum stays constructive as the AI data center supply chain builds supporting infrastructure for the optics transition. No major new product launches broke yesterday, but clear signals emerged from upstream players that demand for CPO-related services and analysis is rising in lockstep with hyperscaler deployment plans.

**Overall Sector View**  
The shift from copper to co-packaged optics continues to advance as a structural necessity for power efficiency and bandwidth scaling in AI factories. NVIDIA and Broadcom remain the primary platform drivers, with TSMC’s advanced packaging technologies (COUPE and related nodes) providing critical manufacturing backbone. Recent volatility in optics stocks appears more sentiment-driven than fundamental, as long-term visibility on 2026-2027 ramps remains intact.

**Key Development**  
Taiwanese material and failure analysis leaders MA-tek and Msscorps posted record Q2 revenues. Growth was fueled by surging demand for advanced process, silicon photonics, and CPO-related services. Both companies highlighted CPO analysis as a high-ASP, customized growth driver and are investing in new equipment to support yield improvement and faster time-to-market for optical-AI chip integration. This reflects tangible ecosystem maturation ahead of broader CPO adoption.

**Major Players Snapshot**  
• **NVIDIA (NVDA)**: Execution on Spectrum-X and Quantum-X CPO platforms continues. Earlier shipments of TSMC-backed switches point to H2 2026 volume ramp-up targeting interconnect and power bottlenecks in large AI clusters.  
• **TSMC (TSM)**: Central foundry and packaging enabler. Rising demand for CPO-related analysis and production support underscores its strategic position in the photonics supply chain.  
• **Broadcom (AVGO)**: Tomahawk 6 / Bailly CPO platform serves as a key Ethernet benchmark. Vertical integration and ecosystem partnerships help address reliability and scaling requirements.

**Supply Chain & Optics Players**  
LITE, COHR, and AAOI continue to track AI infrastructure cycles, with component demand (lasers, engines, interconnects) supporting structural growth. Taiwanese specialists (FOCI/3363, ShunSin/6451) and SIVE benefit indirectly through increased testing, analysis, and packaging activity. Recent moves by analysis providers signal that upstream readiness for higher-volume CPO deployments in switches — and eventual GPU-scale integration — is progressing.

**Core CPO Takeaways**  
• CPO adoption path is methodical: early scale-out switch deployments in 2026, followed by scale-up optical I/O ramps later in the decade. Power savings, density gains, and reduced laser count remain the primary value drivers.  
• Supporting ecosystem (packaging, testing, lasers, analysis) is expanding in parallel, helping de-risk supply chain bottlenecks for hyperscalers.

CPO remains a multi-year structural theme in AI infrastructure. Watch for continued execution signals from platform leaders and incremental supply chain expansions as 2026 volume targets approach. Long-term visibility favors companies enabling the optics transition across the full stack.

#CPO #CoPackagedOptics #AIInfrastructure #Photonics #Semiconductors

Journalism created the discount. Insiders bought it.

Here is what I think the article can have missed. Andrew McKee founded CST now Sivers Photonics in 1999— 27 years ago. He built the photonics business from scratch. Earlier this year Alex McCann appeared publicly as the new MD of Sivers Photonics in April/May communications. McKee moved totechnology role only. His Q1 and Q2 share sales seems to align with this transition and was probably sold (if sold at all in full) parts of it even before share re-rating. My take is that is was done probably with no negative view on the company's prospects. A man monetizing his life's work after almost 30 years of building a company is not insider panic. IMO this is probably more a career ending on his own terms. He deserves it for sure. Even if he sold over 6 months, IMO this is still not a major negative signal. Three things EFN did not check: A) Holdings has known blind spots — transfers to holding companies, ISK structures and family vehicles are not captured. Did anyone verify McKee holds zero shares in any structure? B) One option program was realized in early July. If McKee sold old shares to participate that is not a major negative signal (he is not insider hence would not report sell or buy) — Holdings would not show this yet since it was in early July C) The role change probably happened before most shares was sold, Context matters. Later in the afternoon two board members has publicly shared insider purchases. Todd Thomson — board member, former Citigroup CFO, — bought 535,000 SEK on-market today at 42.80 SEK into the panic. Karin Raj bought 460,000 SEK at 34.68 SEK — the intraday low. Both reported to FI same day. People with more information about the full picture, that also had been part of approving today clarifications around the Q-reports and US Nasdaq listing. Update Vikram CEO have also aquired shares for approximately 1.000.000 SEK Summing up: Journalism created the dip. Insiders bought it. text:
Daily Hard Tech Headlines:

- @ForterraDrive, Caterpillar Inc., IDV, and @Overland_AI_X have been selected for a new U.S. Army program focused on autonomous ground vehicles for battlefield breaching.

- The U.S. Army tested Autonomous Volcano, a @ForterraDrive-enabled driverless truck that can autonomously deploy minefields to block enemy movement.

- Kraken Technology Group and Capewell, with support from the Royal Navy, have successfully airdropped an uncrewed surface vessel from a military cargo aircraft for maritime missions.

- @reservoirfarms, the on-farm agtech and robotics incubator, is expanding into Arizona with access to 500 acres for specialty crop automation testing.

- Kraken Technology Group secured a $175M Series B led by DTCP. 

- @ForterraDrive autonomous ground vehicles have completed 1,100+ frontline missions in Ukraine, with Starlink supporting connectivity.

- Pulse Space has secured a $40M U.S. Space Force award to advance directed-energy infrastructure for space operations, using laser systems to track objects in orbit.

- @MistralAI, the French frontier AI lab, has launched a robotics model focused on warehouse and logistics automation.

- QX LOGISTIX, the Southern California logistics firm, plans to operate 20 Tesla Semi trucks for middle-mile logistics.

- Black Forest Systems has secured a €450,000 funding round. The European startup develops ground and aerial autonomy technology.

- @Leonid_CP, the critical-industry private credit firm, has deployed $6.5M+ to Rx Bandz, which develops compact auto-injectors for emergency and military medicine.

- Leidos has partnered with Rune Technologies to integrate contested logistics technology for predictive military logistics in the Indo-Pacific.

- @fervoenergy, the geothermal technology startup, completed its fastest Cape Station well yet, reaching 19,448 feet in 21 days as drilling rates improved 143% from its first well at the site.

- DOE is deploying up to $40M to support Thea Energy superconducting magnet technology for fusion power and Xscape Photonics Inc photonic computing technology for data centers.

- ispace, inc., the Japanese space company, is now offering SpaceX Starship capacity for lunar payload cargo.

- Talon Precision, Inc. has acquired B&A, an East Coast precision machine shop supporting the aerospace end market.

- @1x_tech, the Bay Area robotics firm, has launched new hands for its NEO humanoid robot, enabling 25 degrees of freedom through a tendon-driven design built for near human-level control.

Laser Photonics Corporation (NASDAQ: LASE) receives a six-figure order from REXA, Inc. for its CleanTech™ IR-3040, a 2kW handheld laser cleaning and surface prep system.
REXA will use it to support actuator surface prep before powder coating.

gigawatts (GW) between 2026 and 2030. > Surging Demand vs. Capped Supply: Global semiconductor team forecasts imply a need for 230+ GW of capacity demand, while the US utilities team expects only 93 GW of accredited supply from regulated utilities. > Massive Compute Load: Driven by AI accelerators, global IT load is expected to require 208 GW between 2026 and 2030. Factoring in a 50% North American share and a 1.20 Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) multiplier to account for cooling and facilities, this translates to 125 GW of direct US data center load growth. > Accelerated Growth Rate: After flat growth from 2010 to 2020 (largely due to a 150bp drag from LED adoption, appliance efficiency, and residential solar), US electrical load is projected to grow at a 4.1% CAGR from 2026 to 2030. > Rise of Gas Engines: Due to turbine scarcity and the need for fast-response grid balancing, data center developers are shifting to gas reciprocating engines. Leading manufacturers like Caterpillar, INNIO Group, Rolls Royce, and Wärtsilä have all announced capacity expansions. > Exponential Rack-Level Power Growth: BofA Global Research pointed out a staggering leap in the power density required by Nvidia's hardware generations. Why do you write this kind of things? You seem to be a knowledge person? I built Sivers from $2m to +$20m for almost 10 years. If you actually know Photonics, as it says in your profile, you should know Sivers, Ayar labs, GF, Coherent, Lumentum, MACOM etc created the CW laser stands before the AI boom. The exact technology now needed for AI.  Ayar labs one off the most respected AI photonics companies chose Sivers lasers before Lumentum and MACOM. Please do your research and be honest. My high growth stocks watchlist 

$PENG $AMD $ASX $LITE $AAOI $VIAV $MXL $MU $SNDK $UCTT $VPG $ICHR $CRDO $ALAB $VICR $STRL 

AI Infrastructure • Semiconductors • Photonics • Power • Data Centers https://t.co/ILQdxFfXq7

Why we've grown to roughly 70 institutional clients in 1 year: 

1. Every name runs through our Three-Layer Cake diligence process. Never a shortcut.

2. Our edge is proprietary operator access, on-the-ground primary work, and non-consensus judgment that generic aggregators can't replicate.

3. Nuclear fuel cycle, uranium mining/enrichment, SMR developers, and photonics/optical interconnects, sectors most generalist shops only cover at the surface.
(Why we focus so heavily on $LITE $SIVE $ASPI $QLE $NNE $COHR ETC.)

4. Risk-adjusted SOTP models, catalyst-tracked theses, and a disciplined report ID system.

5. Tools like our Nuclear Energy Intelligence Terminal give clients forward-looking signal instead of backward-looking synthesis of public documents.

I'd also include our substack, which no true major research companies have, that brings this level of research to retail investors. 

I challenge you to give it a look: https://t.co/lUESQXj0zB

text:
Most investors still see $TSEM as a boring analog foundry.

I think that view is becoming impossible to defend.

Tower now has:

• $1.3B in contracted Silicon Photonics revenue for 2027
• $290M in customer prepayments
• Even larger capacity commitments for 2028
• 5M+ coherent photonic ICs shipped with $MRVL
• A multi-year InP supply agreement with $IQE

This isn’t just another company “exposed” to AI optics.

$TSEM is becoming the manufacturing toll road behind the entire photonics stack.

Lasers, modulators and optical engines can be designed by dozens of companies.

But they still need a foundry capable of manufacturing them at scale.

The deeper I look into AI interconnects, the more convinced I become:

The biggest bottleneck may not be demand.

It may be photonics manufacturing capacity.

Lasers, modulators and optical engines can be designed by dozens of companies. But they still need a foundry capable of manufacturing them at scale. text:
Fren Photon Capital about the "battle" CPO ⚔️ Transceivers:

"Rather than CPO replacing every pluggable, form factors like pluggables, LPO, and CPO are likely to coexist depending on distance and power budget. [For example] NVIDIA is growing its CPO switches and its pluggable ecosystem side by side"

Yes, the CPO growth will be explosive, but the CAGR of the Transceivers will be there to compound for you, don't forget it. 
About the concept of "power budget", I'm more inclined to think it's a concept of COOLING. Even having "free energy" you can't put together a lot of non-CPO photonics connections in high density.

'Photonics' it's not a monolithic thing; it exists different layers inside the chips:

"Switch CPO and XPU-HBM Optical I/O Are Different Contests. Two adjacent architectures often grouped under the CPO and optical-I/O umbrella must be kept apart.

And I add: switch CPO for scale-up is also a different contest than CPO for scale-out and scale-across. That's why Transceivers are also here to stay with us.

text:
J’ai essayé de faire une synthèse de ce qui se passe dans le domaine des Semi ces dernières semaines et pourquoi c’est aussi volatile :

1. le CPO prendrait plus de temps que prévu :
Le scale-up CPO (optique intégrée au plus près des GPU) serait repoussé. Le véritable ramp pourrait finalement intervenir fin 2028 / 2029 plutôt qu'en 2027.

Les principales raisons évoquées :
difficultés      de fabrication,
rendements      insuffisants,
packaging      complexe,
coûts      encore trop élevés.

MAIS attention, ca ne signifie pas que la photonique est morte

Au contraire, cela prolonge la durée de vie de :
800G,
1.6T      pluggables,
LPO,
NPO,
CW      lasers,
cuivre      actif (AEC).

Petit parenthèse pour Sivers : 
Même si le gros ramp est repoussé de quelques trimestres, cela laisse justement plus de temps à Sivers pour :
augmenter      sa capacité de production,
améliorer      les rendements,
obtenir      les qualifications,
signer de      nouveaux contrats,
sécuriser      davantage de clients.

Les hyperscalers ne vont pas attendre 2028 pour commencer à qualifier leurs fournisseurs.

Les capacités industrielles doivent être prêtes avant les volumes.
À mon sens, cela n'empêche absolument pas de voir de nombreuses bonnes nouvelles en 2026-2027.
 Mais comme d’hab, l’exécution doit suivre

2. Rumeurs de retard Rubin
Plusieurs rumeurs évoquaient un retard de Rubin.

À ce stade :
aucune      confirmation officielle,
Nvidia      continue de communiquer normalement,
plusieurs      sources ont contesté ces rumeurs.
Prudence donc avant de considérer un retard majeur comme acquis.

3 .Rapport B. Riley sur AAOI
B. Riley estime que les nouvelles architectures réseau (Amazon RNG, OpenAI MRC) pourraient réduire le nombre de liens optiques nécessaires.
Leur scénario :
moins de      switches,
moins de      transceivers,
donc      moins de demande pour des sociétés comme AAOI.
C'est probablement la note la plus baissière de la semaine.
 MAIS cela a été contredit par de nombreuses personnes :

On a vu : Rosenblatt , Goldman Sachs,  Morgan Stanley,  Photon Capital,  plusieurs anciens ingénieurs réseaux dire à peu près : 
oui les architectures évoluent, mais non cela ne détruit pas la demande optique.
 Pourquoi ? Parce que B.Riley raisonne surtout sur  nombre de liens.
Les autres répondent : oui, mais :
les liens      deviennent beaucoup plus rapides
plus chers , plus  complexes ,plus de contenu optique
plus de coherent  , plus de  lasers , plus de DSP ,plus de CW lasers

Donc le nombre de ports peut diminuer...sans que le chiffre d'affaires du secteur baisse.

Maintenant pour les nouvelles positives :
Goldman Sachs
Continue de voir :
Optical Devices : Very Tight
Optical Cables : Very Tight
jusqu'en 2027.
Autrement dit :
malgré toutes les nouvelles capacités de production, la demande resterait supérieure à l'offre.

Rosenblatt , on l'a vu reste très positif sur AAOI en particulier 
Reste très positif.
Ils continuent notamment d'anticiper pour AAOI :
Beat
Raise
Guidance Q3 proche de 300 M$
Ils considèrent que les vendeurs à découvert exploitent surtout la peur actuelle.

Morgan Stanley
Morgan Stanley reste positif sur le thème IA.
Ils mettent surtout en avant les véritables bottlenecks 
mémoire,
énergie,
photonique,
interconnexions,
power delivery.

aussi volatile : 1. le CPO prendrait plus de temps que prévu : Le scale-up CPO (optique intégrée au plus près des GPU) serait repoussé. Le véritable ramp pourrait finalement intervenir fin 2028 / 2029 plutôt qu'en 2027. Les principales raisons évoquées : difficultés de fabrication, rendements insuffisants, packaging complexe, coûts encore trop élevés. MAIS attention, ca ne signifie pas que la photonique est morte Au contraire, cela prolonge la durée de vie de : 800G, 1.6T pluggables, LPO, NPO, CW lasers, cuivre actif (AEC). Petit parenthèse pour Sivers : Même si le gros ramp est repoussé de quelques trimestres, cela laisse justement plus de temps à Sivers pour : augmenter sa capacité de production, améliorer les rendements, obtenir les qualifications, signer de nouveaux contrats, sécuriser davantage de clients. Les hyperscalers ne vont pas attendre 2028 pour commencer à qualifier leurs fournisseurs. Les capacités industrielles doivent être prêtes avant les volumes. À mon sens, cela n'empêche absolument pas de voir de nombreuses bonnes nouvelles en 2026-2027. Mais comme d’hab, l’exécution doit suivre 2. Rumeurs de retard Rubin Plusieurs rumeurs évoquaient un retard de Rubin. À ce stade : aucune confirmation officielle, Nvidia continue de communiquer normalement, plusieurs sources ont contesté ces rumeurs. Prudence donc avant de considérer un retard majeur comme acquis. 3 .Rapport B. Riley sur AAOI B. Riley estime que les nouvelles architectures réseau (Amazon RNG, OpenAI MRC) pourraient réduire le nombre de liens optiques nécessaires. Leur scénario : moins de switches, moins de transceivers, donc moins de demande pour des sociétés comme AAOI. C'est probablement la note la plus baissière de la semaine. MAIS cela a été contredit par de nombreuses personnes : On a vu : Rosenblatt , Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Photon Capital, plusieurs anciens ingénieurs réseaux dire à peu près : oui les architectures évoluent, mais non cela ne détruit pas la demande optique. Pourquoi ? Parce que B.Riley raisonne surtout sur nombre de liens. Les autres répondent : oui, mais : les liens deviennent beaucoup plus rapides plus chers , plus complexes ,plus de contenu optique plus de coherent , plus de lasers , plus de DSP ,plus de CW lasers Donc le nombre de ports peut diminuer...sans que le chiffre d'affaires du secteur baisse. Maintenant pour les nouvelles positives : Goldman Sachs Continue de voir : Optical Devices : Very Tight Optical Cables : Very Tight jusqu'en 2027. Autrement dit : malgré toutes les nouvelles capacités de production, la demande resterait supérieure à l'offre. Rosenblatt , on l'a vu reste très positif sur AAOI en particulier Reste très positif. Ils continuent notamment d'anticiper pour AAOI : Beat Raise Guidance Q3 proche de 300 M$ Ils considèrent que les vendeurs à découvert exploitent surtout la peur actuelle. Morgan Stanley Morgan Stanley reste positif sur le thème IA. Ils mettent surtout en avant les véritables bottlenecks mémoire, énergie, photonique, interconnexions, power delivery. #AI #ArtificialIntelligence #AIInfrastructure #Datacenter #Photonics #SiliconPhotonics #Semiconductors #Optics #CPO #NPO #LPO #800G #1600G #Networking #Hyperscalers #SIVE #AAOI #MRVL #LITE #COHR"}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 1, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2076334054924836978", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "60", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
TSMC Leads the First Wave of CPO, While Samsung Bets on the Next Generation of AI Packaging

The race for co-packaged optics (CPO) is entering a new phase, and two semiconductor giants are taking very different approaches.

$TSM currently leads the market with real commercial deployments, while Samsung is betting on a more advanced AI packaging architecture that could become important later in the decade.

TSMC Has the Early Lead

TSMC has already moved beyond the development stage into commercial deployment.

Its silicon photonics and advanced packaging technologies are powering next-generation CPO switches from:

$AVGO – 102.4Tbps Ethernet switch built on TSMC’s COUPE silicon photonics platform.
$NVDA – Quantum-X photonic switches are already shipping, while Spectrum-X enters production.

Early customers include $CRWV, Lambda, and $ORCL.

These products integrate optical engines alongside networking ASICs to improve bandwidth while reducing power consumption.

Samsung Is Targeting the Next Opportunity

Rather than competing directly in today’s switch market, Samsung is developing its 2.xD advanced packaging platform.

The goal is to integrate:

High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM)
Logic chips
Silicon photonics

into a single package.

This architecture targets the next generation of AI accelerators, where optical I/O moves directly into GPU and AI compute packages instead of remaining inside networking switches.

Why Optical I/O Matters

As AI models continue growing, power efficiency has become just as important as raw computing performance.

Moving optical connections closer to AI chips dramatically reduces power consumption:

Traditional pluggable optics: ~10 pJ per bit
Switch-level CPO: ~5 pJ per bit
Optical I/O integrated beside AI chips: ~2 pJ per bit

Lower power consumption means lower operating costs and higher AI system performance.

Samsung’s Biggest Advantage

Samsung has one unique strength that few competitors can match.

It owns technologies across the entire AI stack:

Memory (HBM)
Logic foundry
Silicon photonics
Advanced packaging

This vertical integration could eventually allow Samsung to optimize every major component inside future AI systems.

But Execution Remains the Challenge

Integrating multiple advanced chips into one package also creates major manufacturing challenges.

Combining GPUs, HBM, photonic chips, electrical chips, and advanced interposers significantly increases packaging complexity.

If any single chip fails during manufacturing, the entire package may need to be discarded, making production yields the biggest hurdle.

Meanwhile, competitors continue investing aggressively.

$TSM is expanding CoWoS and silicon photonics.
$SKHY is building a $3.87 billion advanced packaging facility in Indiana and expanding its own CPO capabilities.

Investor Takeaway

Today, TSMC is the clear commercial leader, with real products, customer deployments, and production already underway.

Samsung’s strategy is more ambitious. If demand shifts toward fully integrated AI packages combining GPUs, HBM, and optical interconnects, its vertically integrated model could become a powerful competitive advantage.

For investors, the biggest catalyst isn’t technology roadmaps—it’s customer orders. The first major AI customer to adopt a fully integrated HBM + logic + optical I/O package could determine the next leader in AI infrastructure.

Stocks to watch: $TSM $NVDA $AVGO $SKHY $MU $CRWV $ORCL $MRVL

"urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzYzMjIzMTIwMzU2NjM4NzI=", "text": "TSMC Leads the First Wave of CPO, While Samsung Bets on the Next Generation of AI Packaging The race for co-packaged optics (CPO) is entering a new phase, and two semiconductor giants are taking very different approaches. $TSM currently leads the market with real commercial deployments, while Samsung is betting on a more advanced AI packaging architecture that could become important later in the decade. TSMC Has the Early Lead TSMC has already moved beyond the development stage into commercial deployment. Its silicon photonics and advanced packaging technologies are powering next-generation CPO switches from: $AVGO – 102.4Tbps Ethernet switch built on TSMC’s COUPE silicon photonics platform. $NVDA – Quantum-X photonic switches are already shipping, while Spectrum-X enters production. Early customers include $CRWV, Lambda, and $ORCL. These products integrate optical engines alongside networking ASICs to improve bandwidth while reducing power consumption. Samsung Is Targeting the Next Opportunity Rather than competing directly in today’s switch market, Samsung is developing its 2.xD advanced packaging platform. The goal is to integrate: High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) Logic chips Silicon photonics into a single package. This architecture targets the next generation of AI accelerators, where optical I/O moves directly into GPU and AI compute packages instead of remaining inside networking switches. Why Optical I/O Matters As AI models continue growing, power efficiency has become just as important as raw computing performance. Moving optical connections closer to AI chips dramatically reduces power consumption: Traditional pluggable optics: ~10 pJ per bit Switch-level CPO: ~5 pJ per bit Optical I/O integrated beside AI chips: ~2 pJ per bit Lower power consumption means lower operating costs and higher AI system performance. Samsung’s Biggest Advantage Samsung has one unique strength that few competitors can match. It owns technologies across the entire AI stack: Memory (HBM) Logic foundry Silicon photonics Advanced packaging This vertical integration could eventually allow Samsung to optimize every major component inside future AI systems. But Execution Remains the Challenge Integrating multiple advanced chips into one package also creates major manufacturing challenges. Combining GPUs, HBM, photonic chips, electrical chips, and advanced interposers significantly increases packaging complexity. If any single chip fails during manufacturing, the entire package may need to be discarded, making production yields the biggest hurdle. Meanwhile, competitors continue investing aggressively. $TSM is expanding CoWoS and silicon photonics. $SKHY is building a $3.87 billion advanced packaging facility in Indiana and expanding its own CPO capabilities. Investor Takeaway Today, TSMC is the clear commercial leader, with real products, customer deployments, and production already underway. Samsung’s strategy is more ambitious. If demand shifts toward fully integrated AI packages combining GPUs, HBM, and optical interconnects, its vertically integrated model could become a powerful competitive advantage. For investors, the biggest catalyst isn’t technology roadmaps—it’s customer orders. The first major AI customer to adopt a fully integrated HBM + logic + optical I/O package could determine the next leader in AI infrastructure. Stocks to watch: $TSM $NVDA $AVGO $SKHY $MU $CRWV $ORCL $MRVL"}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "core": {"user_results": {"result": {"__typename": "User", "affiliates_highlighted_label": {}, "avatar": {"image_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1897334033614274560/7YWKk1Uh_normal.jpg"}, "core": {"created_at": "Mon Apr 08 00:14:38 +0000 2019", "name": "WealthBranch", "screen_name": "WealthBranch"}, "dm_permissions": {"can_dm": false}, "follow_request_sent": false, "has_graduated_access": true, "id": "VXNlcjoxMTE1MDQ1MjQ2ODA1NzA4ODAw", "is_blue_verified": true, "legacy": {"default_profile": true, "default_profile_image": false, "description": "Find swing trading setups in a few swipes. text:
Agentic AI is starting to do the work of a full scouting team, now in private markets. @earlysignalsvc  points it at private tech companies and scores them the week they raise, launch, or leave stealth, not the week they trend.

The whole edge in markets is acting while a signal is still fresh, before everyone else has read the same headline. Public equities are already a crowded place to try that, which is the game the DeepSeek Portfolio plays every week. Doing it in venture, at the moment a company raises or leaves stealth, is a much earlier point on the curve.

Forty-four companies ranked this week, each on six scored dimensions plus a potential and a scoop score. Photonics for AI data centers, infrastructure for long-running agents, on-device writing tools. Systematic scoring pushed all the way to the front of the funnel.

Good work. Worth a look.

EarlySignals scores private tech companies the week they raise, launch, or come out of stealth. 

44 companies, ranked this week.
Four from the open sample, and why they stood out. https://t.co/M2jF1xj7G2

text:
This post from relatively unknown account @0xBADB01E is going viral within the AI community - here's why

The core claim is genuinely contrarian and flips how we think about scaling architectures and supply chain constraints: 

> premise: the wires between chips matter more than the chips themselves or the memory attached to them

> if you designed an AI inference machine starting from the interconnect and working backwards, you'd build something radically different (and cheaper) than an Nvidia GPU

> GPUs are terrible at generating tokens - when an LLM generates text one token at a time (decode), a Blackwell GPU uses under 20% of its math capability as the compute units sit idle waiting for weights to arrive from memory

IOW, you bought a Ferrari engine and it spends 80% of its time waiting at the gas pump

> the intuitive answer is "add faster memory" (more HBM) but the smarter answer is: since a single chip can't have infinite memory bandwidth, split the model across many chips and let each chip handle a slice

> the problem then shifts because the constraint is no longer memory bandwidth, it's how fast chips can talk to each other (the interconnect)

> Nvidia's interconnect is the wrong approach - NVLink is a firehose with huge bandwidth, but high latency (delay per message)

> that was a deliberate choice for training, but not ideal for inference where the data crossing between chips per token is absurdly tiny 

It's like paying a $50 shipping-and-handling fee on a $1 product

The contrarian take is that everyone says "decode is memory-bandwidth bound," but once you shard the model, per-user speed is gated by how quickly the tiny inter-chip messages complete, not by how fat the memory pipe is - decode is latency-bound, not bandwidth-bound

> if latency is what matters, you can build around slow, cheap memory (LPDDR instead of HBM), because its access latency is about the same as HBM's; only its bandwidth is worse

> then you only need enough FLOPs to match that memory

The above two statements have big implications:

1. this is a direct thesis against HBM's centrality in the compute buildout

2. the packaging supply chain that's currently the binding constraint on Nvidia's output matters much less

3. for photonics, it can potentially go either way - depends on if optical is focused on improving bandwidth (bearish) or latency (bullish)

I don't know this account but this post was interesting and intelligent so I'm here for the conversation that follows

As an example,", "id_str": "2073990357398982797", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "0xBADB01E", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "2073970411138949533", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "1811787044722479105", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 55, "reply_count": 136, "retweet_count": 192, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "1811787044722479105"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzM5OTAzNTcxNzI1NTE2ODA=", "text": "First you have to understand that modern LLM inference already disaggregates weights as models outgrew single chips years ago. You shard either by layer (pipeline parallelism) or by slicing every layer (tensor parallelism), and the two do very different things. As an example, let’s look at Llama 3.3. It has 70B of weights and at FP8 that’s 70 GB of memory which is enough to fit on a single H100. Now that H100 has 3.35 TB/s of HBM, so the fastest it can ever decode for one user is 70/3.35 ≈ 21 ms/token or ~48 tok/s while using under 1% of its FLOPs. Now if we pipeline it across 8 chips: each chip holds ~8.75 GB, which means it only needs 1/8th the bandwidth and 1/8th the FLOPs to sustain the same aggregate throughput. Now crucially the token/sec a user gets is limited by the amount of data that crosses the link. In current LLMs all that is a small amount of activations for LLama 3.3 it’s ~8 KB per token…. Yes, you read that right it’s 8 KILOBYTES we are sending over a <900 GB/s link. That’s only 9 ns of serialization time but the overhead of 224G PAM4 SerDes adds ~100 ns per link traversal with RS-FEC which is 11x longer than the payload itself. And then you have the NVSwitch adding ~300 ns per hop and you need to pay twice. That’s ~600 ns of just hardware latency wrapped around 9 ns of data making a 98% tax before software even shows up. Then NCCL’s collective stack turns 600 ns into 10-20+ us… all to move 8 kilobytes lol. For comparison 8 KB serializes over 10 Gigabit Ethernet NRZ, in just 6.6 us. Pipeline parallelism however doesn’t make a single user faster as the token still needs to visit every layer in the sequence, so per-user speed is still weights / per-chip bandwidth. To get more speed per user token you need to use tensor parallelism and have all the chips work on the same layer simultaneously. TP costs you 2 all reduce OPs per layer, 160 per token on llama 3, that’s still kilobytes of traffic but with NVLink overhead it’s a massive tax and why pipeline parallelism on most models still gives more interactivity per user. However, this gives you a huge latency lever to pull that scales tokens per second with interconnect speed instead of memory BW. The clever amongst you might have also realized that sharding doesn’t just cut memory bandwidth per chip it also cuts FLOPs per chip and is why we have such bad MFU on decode. So once you’ve sized the link for the memory, you need to size the compute for it too. This is called “balancing the pipeline”, and currently no shipping chip does it because they were all designed as standalone monsters. Remember Tokens/sec = ~aggregate memory BW / bytes touched per token. At batch 64 in FP4 you need ~250 FLOPs per byte, and Blackwell ships 1,250. Provisioned 5x more than the narrow pipe of HBM. Nobody saturates shit cause they are all building around HBM. So now it all comes full circle. Parallelism reduces memory bw pressure and thus FLOPs but increases interconnect latency pressure. Despite having HBM and GigaSERDES we aren’t actually doing more work lol. But if you really wanted to balance the pipeline you need to match the memory bandwidth, the flops, and most importantly the interconnect. So what does that look like ? Well if you build around LPDDR’s lower bandwidth, lower your interconnect latency, you actually can beat Nvidia on decode with a fraction of the silicon."}}}, "rest_id": "2073990357398982797", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1494678", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2074201112006242337", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "5104", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

$BURU @NUBURUlasers is extending its laser platform from counter-UAS to underwater autonomy.

Through Lyocon Italian laser-tech factory and photonics center of excellence, NUBURU is working with Suncubes to connect blue-laser technology, beam-control and optical power transmission into new Defense & Security applications.

✅Recent PRs highlight two strategic directions:
- Laser Arm: vehicle-integrated directed-energy and counter-UAS systems.
- DEEP LIGHT: blue-laser power-beaming for underwater autonomy, subsea power and maritime infrastructure.

The opportunity is not only one project. It is the potential validation of NUBURU blue-laser technology in mission-critical environments where power, endurance and optical performance matter.
Lyocon provides the laser engine. 
☀️SunCubes provides beam-control and optical power capabilities. NUBURU builds the platform.

Subject to definitive agreements, technical validation, regulatory clearances and applicable approvals.

text:
amazing!

"MIT researchers developed 2 new photonic coupling technologies, the Evanescent Coupler and GRIN Coupler, that could enable future chip packages to exceed 1 petabit per second (1,000 Tb/s) of communication bandwidth."

"Instead of sending data through power hungry copper wires, the system uses light (photonics) to move information between chips, dramatically increasing bandwidth while reducing energy consumption and heat."

"Researchers designed the technology to be compatible with existing CMOS semiconductor manufacturing, potentially making ultra-high bandwidth photonic chip packages practical for future AI accelerators, supercomputers and hyperscale data centers."

text:
There's a company almost no one is talking about, trading near NAV, that sells into three of the hottest markets right now: AI chipmaking, defense batteries, and photonics.

And it has several unfair advantages:
→ $100M Department of Energy grant.
→ Samsung SDI building and backing its battery factory.
→ A 2028 law that bans the US military from buying Chinese battery cells.

That company is @ForgeNano, and the vehicle taking it public at a $1.2B valuation is Archimedes Tech SPAC Partners II $ATII, run by the same team that took SoundHound $SOUN public in 2022.

Forge Nano sells atomic-scale coatings, protective layers thousands of times thinner than a human hair, that make batteries last longer and chips fail less.

$ATII trades at $10.88/share today against a trust worth ~$10.63/share, so the downside from here is ~2%. And you can buy $ATIIW if you want the leveraged upside via warrants.

I've done deep dives (and bought) into SPAC setups like this before:
→ Boost Run $BRUN pre-merger (originally $WLAC).
→ Exascale Labs $BCAR, still pre-merger.

My full 6k-word deep dive on $ATII will be released tomorrow. Link in bio to read it when it comes out!

Thanks to @SPAC_zilla and @stepnotonpets for the idea!

text:
AI 사이클 내년이후로 길게보면
엔비디아 TSMC 브로드컴
지표기업들
컴퓨팅파워와 속도 네트워킹.
ㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡ

[뉴스] TSMC의 PIC 생산 능력, 2028년까지 월 25,000장으로 급증 전망; 엔비디아와 브로드컴이 초기 COUPE 고객으로 거론됨

TSMC가 실리콘 포토닉스(Silicon Photonics) 분야로 사업을 확장하면서 시장의 관심이 쏠리고 있는 가운데, PIC(광집적회로, Photonic Integrated Circuits) 웨이퍼 생산 능력이 대폭 확대될 것으로 예상됩니다. 상업시보(Commercial Times)에 따르면, 기관 투자자들은 TSMC의 PIC 생산 능력이 월 약 500장 수준에서 2026년 2분기 10,000장으로 늘어나고, 4분기에는 15,000장으로 추가 확대되며, 2028년에는 월 최소 25,000장에 달할 것으로 추산하고 있습니다.

해당 보도에서 강조했듯, 초기 생산 램프업(생산량 확대) 기간 동안에는 PIC 생산 능력이 제한적이기 때문에 2026~2027년 TSMC의 COUPE 플랫폼 양산을 이용할 주요 고객은 엔비디아(NVIDIA), 브로드컴(Broadcom), AMD가 될 가능성이 높습니다. 2028년에 생산 능력이 더욱 확대되면, 미디어텍(MediaTek), 마벨(Marvell), 아야 랩스(Ayar Labs) 등 고객사의 CPO(공동 패키지 광학, Co-Packaged Optics) 프로젝트 역시 TSMC의 양산 플랫폼에 진입할 수 있을 것으로 보입니다.

PIC는 전기 및 광 신호를 변환, 라우팅, 결합하는 역할을 담당합니다. AI 서버 클러스터가 지속적으로 확장되고 스위치 대역폭이 25T 및 50T에서 100T 및 200T로 고도화됨에 따라 광 엔진에 대한 수요도 증가하고 있으며, 이에 따라 TSMC의 COUPE 플랫폼 진행 상황이 시장의 주요 관심사가 되고 있다고 보도는 덧붙였습니다.

이 보도에 따르면, 웨이퍼당 648개의 다이(die)가 생산된다고 가정할 때, TSMC의 월간 PIC 생산 능력이 500장에서 10,000장으로 늘어나면 연간 PIC 생산량은 약 400만 개에서 7,800만 개로 급증하게 됩니다. 생산 능력이 월 25,000장으로 더욱 확대될 경우, 연간 PIC 생산량은 약 1억 9,400만 개에 달할 수 있습니다.

이러한 전망을 바탕으로, 해당 보도는 TSMC의 PIC 생산 능력 확대가 갖는 세 가지 주요 시사점을 강조합니다.

1. CPO의 양산 진입: 
CPO 기술이 실험 및 소량 검증 단계를 넘어 점차 대량 생산 준비 단계로 나아가고 있음을 의미합니다.

2. 포괄적 AI 플랫폼 구축: 
실리콘 포토닉스와 첨단 패키징(Advanced Packaging)의 결합을 통해 COUPE, SoIC, CoWoS를 모두 결합한 더욱 포괄적인 AI 광전(optoelectronic) 플랫폼이 구축될 것으로 예상됩니다.

3. 관련 장비 수요 증가: 
PIC 생산량이 증가함에 따라 광섬유 어레이 유닛(FAU), 레이저, 광학 테스트 장비, 프로브 카드(Probe cards), 테스트 소켓 및 자동화 장비에 대한 수요를 견인할 것으로 예상됩니다.

상용화를 향해 나아가는 TSMC의 COUPE 플랫폼
경제일보(Economic Daily News)의 보도처럼, TSMC는 자사의 COUPE 플랫폼을 기반으로 한 세계 최초의 200Gbps 마이크로 링 변조기(MRM, Micro-Ring Modulator)가 2026년 하반기에 양산에 들어갈 예정이라고 발표했습니다. 
또한 TSMC는 이 소자가 공정 제어 조건 하에서 1E-08 미만의 비트 에러율(Bit Error Rate)을 달성했다고 밝혔습니다.

그러나 상업시보는 PIC 생산 능력 증가가 곧바로 CPO의 대규모 양산 램프업을 의미하는 것은 아니라고 지적합니다. 

다운스트림(후공정) 프로세스가 완성되기 위해서는 SoIC 통합, 광전 테스트, 광 엔진 패키징, FAU 결합, 시스템 수준의 검증 등 여러 단계를 아직 통과해야 하기 때문입니다.

월 25,000장으로 급증 전망; 엔비디아와 브로드컴이 초기 COUPE 고객으로 거론됨 TSMC가 실리콘 포토닉스(Silicon Photonics) 분야로 사업을 확장하면서 시장의 관심이 쏠리고 있는 가운데, PIC(광집적회로, Photonic Integrated Circuits) 웨이퍼 생산 능력이 대폭 확대될 것으로 예상됩니다. 상업시보(Commercial Times)에 따르면, 기관 투자자들은 TSMC의 PIC 생산 능력이 월 약 500장 수준에서 2026년 2분기 10,000장으로 늘어나고, 4분기에는 15,000장으로 추가 확대되며, 2028년에는 월 최소 25,000장에 달할 것으로 추산하고 있습니다. 해당 보도에서 강조했듯, 초기 생산 램프업(생산량 확대) 기간 동안에는 PIC 생산 능력이 제한적이기 때문에 2026~2027년 TSMC의 COUPE 플랫폼 양산을 이용할 주요 고객은 엔비디아(NVIDIA), 브로드컴(Broadcom), AMD가 될 가능성이 높습니다. 2028년에 생산 능력이 더욱 확대되면, 미디어텍(MediaTek), 마벨(Marvell), 아야 랩스(Ayar Labs) 등 고객사의 CPO(공동 패키지 광학, Co-Packaged Optics) 프로젝트 역시 TSMC의 양산 플랫폼에 진입할 수 있을 것으로 보입니다. PIC는 전기 및 광 신호를 변환, 라우팅, 결합하는 역할을 담당합니다. AI 서버 클러스터가 지속적으로 확장되고 스위치 대역폭이 25T 및 50T에서 100T 및 200T로 고도화됨에 따라 광 엔진에 대한 수요도 증가하고 있으며, 이에 따라 TSMC의 COUPE 플랫폼 진행 상황이 시장의 주요 관심사가 되고 있다고 보도는 덧붙였습니다. 이 보도에 따르면, 웨이퍼당 648개의 다이(die)가 생산된다고 가정할 때, TSMC의 월간 PIC 생산 능력이 500장에서 10,000장으로 늘어나면 연간 PIC 생산량은 약 400만 개에서 7,800만 개로 급증하게 됩니다. 생산 능력이 월 25,000장으로 더욱 확대될 경우, 연간 PIC 생산량은 약 1억 9,400만 개에 달할 수 있습니다. 이러한 전망을 바탕으로, 해당 보도는 TSMC의 PIC 생산 능력 확대가 갖는 세 가지 주요 시사점을 강조합니다. 1. CPO의 양산 진입: CPO 기술이 실험 및 소량 검증 단계를 넘어 점차 대량 생산 준비 단계로 나아가고 있음을 의미합니다. 2. 포괄적 AI 플랫폼 구축: 실리콘 포토닉스와 첨단 패키징(Advanced Packaging)의 결합을 통해 COUPE, SoIC, CoWoS를 모두 결합한 더욱 포괄적인 AI 광전(optoelectronic) 플랫폼이 구축될 것으로 예상됩니다. 3. 관련 장비 수요 증가: PIC 생산량이 증가함에 따라 광섬유 어레이 유닛(FAU), 레이저, 광학 테스트 장비, 프로브 카드(Probe cards), 테스트 소켓 및 자동화 장비에 대한 수요를 견인할 것으로 예상됩니다. 상용화를 향해 나아가는 TSMC의 COUPE 플랫폼 경제일보(Economic Daily News)의 보도처럼, TSMC는 자사의 COUPE 플랫폼을 기반으로 한 세계 최초의 200Gbps 마이크로 링 변조기(MRM, Micro-Ring Modulator)가 2026년 하반기에 양산에 들어갈 예정이라고 발표했습니다. 또한 TSMC는 이 소자가 공정 제어 조건 하에서 1E-08 미만의 비트 에러율(Bit Error Rate)을 달성했다고 밝혔습니다. 그러나 상업시보는 PIC 생산 능력 증가가 곧바로 CPO의 대규모 양산 램프업을 의미하는 것은 아니라고 지적합니다. 다운스트림(후공정) 프로세스가 완성되기 위해서는 SoIC 통합, 광전 테스트, 광 엔진 패키징, FAU 결합, 시스템 수준의 검증 등 여러 단계를 아직 통과해야 하기 때문입니다. https://t.co/w4FQ8IuzkB"}}}, "rest_id": "2074927905860427952", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "539", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🇨🇦 Canada is quietly becoming one of the world’s biggest quantum computing hubs, and investors are starting to pay attention.

For years, Canada invested heavily in quantum research while much of the market focused on AI. Now that companies like $NVDA are openly embracing quantum computing, investors are beginning to realize that Canada may already have one of the strongest quantum ecosystems outside the U.S.

Since 2002, Canada has invested more than $2 billion into quantum technology, while the government recently committed another $334 million to accelerate development. That funding isn’t just supporting research—it’s helping build an entire industry spanning hardware, software, cybersecurity, networking, and future AI infrastructure.

One of the biggest names is $XNDU (Xanadu Quantum Technologies), which recently became Canada’s first publicly traded pure-play quantum computing company.

Unlike many competitors building superconducting quantum computers, Xanadu is developing photonic quantum computers, which use light (photons) instead of electrical circuits. Many researchers believe photonics could eventually offer better scalability, lower cooling requirements, and easier integration into future AI data centers.

Canada’s strength extends well beyond one company.

Companies like $BTQ, Quantum eMotion, Photonic, Nord Quantique, and Quantum Technologies are all building different pieces of the future quantum ecosystem, from post-quantum cybersecurity to fault-tolerant quantum hardware.

A major validation came when DARPA, the U.S. Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, selected several Canadian quantum startups for its Quantum Benchmarking Initiative. DARPA is looking for technologies capable of building commercially useful quantum computers by 2033.

That recognition suggests Canadian companies are competing on a global stage—not just in academic research.

Why is $XNDU heavily shorted?

Despite the excitement, $XNDU currently has one of the highest short interests in the market, with over 70% of its free float sold short.

Short sellers believe the company’s valuation has moved far ahead of today’s business fundamentals. Revenue remains relatively small, profitability is still years away, and commercial quantum computing is still in its early stages.

Their argument is simple:

“The technology is promising—but investors may be paying tomorrow’s price for a company that is still building today’s products.”

However, the opposite argument is equally compelling.

If $XNDU continues hitting technical milestones, signs major commercial partnerships, or demonstrates meaningful progress toward scalable quantum computing, that extremely high short interest could fuel a powerful short squeeze, forcing bearish investors to buy back shares at much higher prices.

Wall Street remains constructive despite the volatility, with analysts maintaining a Moderate Buy rating and an average price target implying roughly 275% upside from recent trading levels.

The bigger picture

Think about AI 15 years ago.

Back then, most AI companies generated little revenue and many looked expensive. Investors who focused only on current earnings often missed the enormous opportunity that followed.

Quantum computing could be following a similar path.

Today it’s still experimental.

Over the next decade, it could transform drug discovery, financial modeling, materials science, cybersecurity, climate simulation, and AI itself.

There will likely be winners and losers along the way, but Canada has quietly positioned itself as one of the global leaders in this race.

For long-term investors, the real question isn’t whether quantum computing will matter.

It’s which companies will become the Nvidia, TSMC, or Microsoft of the quantum era. $XNDU is one of the companies trying to earn that position, making it one of the more closely watched—and most controversial—quantum stocks in the market today.

For years, Canada invested heavily in quantum research while much of the market focused on AI. Now that companies like $NVDA are openly embracing quantum computing, investors are beginning to realize that Canada may already have one of the strongest quantum ecosystems outside the U.S. Since 2002, Canada has invested more than $2 billion into quantum technology, while the government recently committed another $334 million to accelerate development. That funding isn’t just supporting research—it’s helping build an entire industry spanning hardware, software, cybersecurity, networking, and future AI infrastructure. One of the biggest names is $XNDU (Xanadu Quantum Technologies), which recently became Canada’s first publicly traded pure-play quantum computing company. Unlike many competitors building superconducting quantum computers, Xanadu is developing photonic quantum computers, which use light (photons) instead of electrical circuits. Many researchers believe photonics could eventually offer better scalability, lower cooling requirements, and easier integration into future AI data centers. Canada’s strength extends well beyond one company. Companies like $BTQ, Quantum eMotion, Photonic, Nord Quantique, and Quantum Technologies are all building different pieces of the future quantum ecosystem, from post-quantum cybersecurity to fault-tolerant quantum hardware. A major validation came when DARPA, the U.S. Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, selected several Canadian quantum startups for its Quantum Benchmarking Initiative. DARPA is looking for technologies capable of building commercially useful quantum computers by 2033. That recognition suggests Canadian companies are competing on a global stage—not just in academic research. Why is $XNDU heavily shorted? Despite the excitement, $XNDU currently has one of the highest short interests in the market, with over 70% of its free float sold short. Short sellers believe the company’s valuation has moved far ahead of today’s business fundamentals. Revenue remains relatively small, profitability is still years away, and commercial quantum computing is still in its early stages. Their argument is simple: “The technology is promising—but investors may be paying tomorrow’s price for a company that is still building today’s products.” However, the opposite argument is equally compelling. If $XNDU continues hitting technical milestones, signs major commercial partnerships, or demonstrates meaningful progress toward scalable quantum computing, that extremely high short interest could fuel a powerful short squeeze, forcing bearish investors to buy back shares at much higher prices. Wall Street remains constructive despite the volatility, with analysts maintaining a Moderate Buy rating and an average price target implying roughly 275% upside from recent trading levels. The bigger picture Think about AI 15 years ago. Back then, most AI companies generated little revenue and many looked expensive. Investors who focused only on current earnings often missed the enormous opportunity that followed. Quantum computing could be following a similar path. Today it’s still experimental. Over the next decade, it could transform drug discovery, financial modeling, materials science, cybersecurity, climate simulation, and AI itself. There will likely be winners and losers along the way, but Canada has quietly positioned itself as one of the global leaders in this race. For long-term investors, the real question isn’t whether quantum computing will matter. It’s which companies will become the Nvidia, TSMC, or Microsoft of the quantum era. $XNDU is one of the companies trying to earn that position, making it one of the more closely watched—and most controversial—quantum stocks in the market today."}}}, "rest_id": "2074917486324314306", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "312", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
That's right, I don't even know how many posts I've made by now, but my repost to Serenity absolutely had to be there

Totally agree

As I've explained 30 times already, a sale by a former Head of the Photonics branch at $SIVE

Which probably happened months ago, and considering he hasn't even held that position for months, could be due to a thousand reasons

The most likely one being his retirement after 27 years at the company

On the other hand, the purchase by 3 insiders, highlighted by the CEO's 1M SEK buy, is a clear demonstration of confidence

Especially when there were so many doubts regarding the postponement of the earnings report until after the lock-up period on the 16th

Where brainless bears were claiming it was done so they could dump all their shares before $SIVE goes bankrupt

Nothing could be further from the truth

Another thing I want to add is regarding people saying 1M SEK isn't that much for a CEO

That's a lie. You have to understand that $SIVE is a Swedish company, not an American one

I know that in America, CEOs are mega-millionaires making $200k–$500k a year

But in Europe, currency is worth more and prices are lower, so they make much less

So yes, 1M SEK is a lot; it represents several annual salaries for the CEO

And besides, the amount is the least of it, what matters about this buy is what it confirms

It confirms that they believe in the company and its potential to become a key player in the AI supply chain

So yes, this is incredibly good news for $SIVE

Long on $SIVE

Most notably: $SIVEF CEO Vikram bought 1,000,000 SEK worth of shares on the open market today. 

This is following the massive drop on Sivers stock following 2 misleading CPO delay reports, Meta Compute headlines, and another insider

text:
I spent some time reading $AUR latest engineering blog, and one thing really stood out to me.

They’ve built a custom silicon photonic engine for their FirstLight lidar, consolidating light generation, amplification, and detection into a compact multi-chip module.

I don’t think enough people appreciate how important this hardware moat is.
Getting autonomous driving software to work is only half the battle. You have to build hardware that is repeatable, reliable, and cost-effective enough to deploy at scale.

A few key takeaways:
• Fiber-free architecture: They eliminated fragile fiber connectors entirely, routing light through integrated waveguides to survive heavy-duty truck vibrations over hundreds of thousands of miles.

• Automated sub-micron assembly: Instead of slow, manual lab calibration, they use automated active alignment to position microlenses between chips in real-time on the production line.

• Vertical stack ownership: They design everything in-house, down to the epitaxial layers of their lasers, giving them total control over the automotive qualification process (PPAP) and allowing rapid architectural iteration.

As an investor, this is the stuff I pay attention to.

The AI gets all the headlines, but deep hardware engineering decisions like these determine whether a company can actually scale from hundreds of vehicles to tens of thousands.

This is another reason I’m bullish on $AUR and it continues to be my highest conviction moonshot play for the next decade. 

-T.E.E.

text:
WOOOOOOOW

So on the exact same day they try to sink us to death over an alleged sale by the "Head of Photonics $SIVE"

Not only do we recover and turn green

But insiders and the CEO himself bought the dip

All the stupid bears say they are selling

Meanwhile, the numbers show they are doing nothing but buying non stop

I warned you this was the bottom and you dared to laugh at me hahahahah pathetic bears

And now comes the funniest part of all:

When the millionaire positions of all those institutions that were spreading FUD about $SIVE finally come to light

If you still think $SIVE is going to 4 SEK

Instead of realizing that the fundamentals are intact and it was just pure manipulation for institutions to buy lower

Let me tell you, you are not very smart

Long on $SIVE

text:
Creating more energy?
Binary is on or off which takes power to turn on or off.
The most power is consumed when the bit changes state (e.g., flipping from a 0 to a 1).

Is there a lower power way to change state? Like colors or a state change in a lower temp environment?

AI:
Yes, there are much lower-power ways to change a bit's state, and your intuition about using colors and lower temperature environments is spot on with cutting-edge physics and computer engineering.
Modern computers waste a massive amount of energy pushing whole streams of electrons through wires. To bypass this, researchers are developing alternatives based exactly on the concepts you mentioned:
1. Lower Temperature Environments (The Physics Proof)
In physics, there is an absolute fundamental limit to how cheap a state change can be, called the Landauer's principle.
Why temperature matters: Notice the T(temperature) in the equation. Because temperature is directly multiplied by the energy cost, dropping the temperature drastically lowers the minimum energy required to flip a bit. In ultra-cold quantum computing or superconducting environments, the thermal "noise" is so low that bits can change state with a fraction of the energy required at room temperature.

2. Using Colors and Light (Photonics)
Instead of forcing electrons through a silicon transistor, scientists use Optical and Photonic Computing, which relies on light (colors/wavelengths).
Optical Switches: Instead of charging a capacitor with electricity, a pulse of light switches a material between being transparent or reflective.
The Power Advantage: Photons (light particles) do not have mass or electrical charge, meaning they do not generate heat through electrical resistance. Using different wavelengths (colors) allows data states to change at the speed of light with almost zero energy loss. Recent breakthroughs include devices combining magnetism with light to switch states in picoseconds using orders of magnitude less power than traditional electronics.
3. Structural State Changes (Phase-Change & Magnetics)
Beyond light and cold, there are structural ways to rewrite data using minimal energy:
Spintronics (Magnetic Bits): Instead of moving electrons from point A to point B, you simply change the spin (magnetic direction) of an electron right where it sits. This uses almost no moving current and has no capacitive electrical loss.
Phase-Change Memory (PCM): This approach mimics your idea of a physical state change. A tiny electrical pulse switches a glass-like material between an amorphous state (disorganized atoms = 0) and a crystalline state (organized atoms = 1). Once changed, it stays in that state forever without requiring any holding power.

text:
Physical AI pick-and-shovel ⌁ $NOVT DD  🔖🛸

I spend a loooot of time evaluating the physical AI stack... with perhaps a slightly greater focus on humanoids.

Software models are expanding at an extraordinary pace, yet the immediate scaling constraint remains entirely hardware-bound.

Advanced whole-body control policies require instant, flawless translation into the physical world. 
It starts with perception, flows through planning and control, then reaches the physical world through motion, force feedback and actuation.

Every stage compounds error, so micro-second precision and adaptive force feedback determine whether an AI brain can function in reality or fail during contact-rich tasks.

The sim2real gap is a hardware execution problem... 

That's why I keep coming back to Novanta.

The company has built an ecosystem that occupies the exact center of this hardware constraint. 
They deliberately focus on manufacturing the core precision motion, sensing, and also photonics stacks that advanced robotics OEMs require.

🛸 ⌁ Motion stack
Precision motion from Celera Motion and Ingenia supplies the encoders (Aura series and IncOder CORE), frameless and slotless high-torque motors (Omni+ line), and certified servo drives (Denali and Everest Safe equipped with FSoE).

The result is (custom) actuators with sub-micron feedback and cogging-free smoothness paired with deterministic real-time control loops needed for stable locomotion and fluid multi-DOF manipulation. 

🛸 ⌁ Sensing stack
ATI Industrial Automation (one of their greatest acquisition) contributes multi-axis force/torque sensors from the Axia and Varo families together with automatic tool changers and material removal tools that turn position-controlled arms into systems capable of compliant grasping, adaptive manipulation, and importantly safe human interaction. 

These components matter for collision detection in unstructured environments and for force-modulated actions where pure kinematic control falls short.

🛸 ⌁ Photonics stack
The photonics capabilities, built around Cambridge Technology beam steering, Synrad CO2 sources, Laser Quantum solid-state and ultrafast lasers, plus ARGES scanning, support high-precision material processing. 

That matters both for manufacturing the next generation of robots and for the AI-optimized production cells those robots will eventually run inside factories and warehouses.

The laser sources, beam steering, scan heads, and related photonics technologies supplied by Novanta are also used in medical laser procedures, such as ophthalmology, dermatology/plastic surgery, soft tissue applications, and support for minimally invasive/robotic surgery systems.

🛸 ⌁ Healthcare stack (robotics surgery)
Continuing on the medical side, WOM insufflators, NDS visualization systems, but also integrated motion solutions underpin minimally invasive and robotic procedures. 

Riverpoint brings high-growth minimally invasive surgical consumables (like advanced surgical fibers, sutures, and implants for trauma and cardiovascular applications).

As AI moves into semi-autonomous subtasks in surgery to autonomous surgery, the same precision motion and force sensing that serve humanoids translate directly into safer, more consistent tissue interaction and better visualization pipelines, and vice-versa. 

🛸 ⌁ Final thoughts
What stands out is how the pieces fit together.

Acquisitions stitched a full motion and sensing stack, with end-effector tooling and supporting photonics into one offering. 

That integration reduces risk for OEMs who must hit safety and reliability thresholds for real-world deployments. 

Physical AI patform builders still do the system integration and higher-level control. Novanta supplies the subsystems that let those platforms actually perform at the edge of what physics allows.

$NOVT
